prompt: can you write a basketball coach pep talk for kids who are about to water the houseplants
response: Those plants cannot ask for water. They cannot dribble. They cannot shoot. But they can thrive — if you show up for them. Get the can. Fill it. Don't rush the fern. The fern has been waiting. Let's go.
intent: Encourage kids to water plants using the motivational language of a basketball coach.

prompt: what does it mean to dream that every app on my phone had a badge number that kept going up
response: Accumulating badges with no ceiling describe a quantified guilt — each number is a small unchosen obligation. The fact that they only increase and never clear suggests the dream has modeled a system in which completion is not structurally possible. This is less about the apps and more about a life in which the tally never resets.
intent: Examine a pervasive sense that responsibilities compound faster than they can ever be discharged.

prompt: flashcard on how to handle receiving a compliment without deflecting or gloating
response:
FRONT: Someone compliments your work. Your options are to dismiss it or oversell it. What's the third move?

BACK: Receive it briefly and specifically. "Thank you — I'm glad the structure worked for you." This accepts the gift without inflating it. Deflection denies reality; gloating borrows against future goodwill. Specificity signals you're actually listening.
intent: Teach a calibrated response to positive feedback that avoids both false modesty and overclaiming.

prompt: Write a coupon for a discount on a dementia care facility assessment
response:
MEMORY LANE SAVINGS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
20% OFF
Memory Care Facility Assessment

Code: PRESENTMOMENT
Valid for new residents.
Facility tour included at no charge.
intent: Create a promotional offer for evaluating appropriate care options for cognitive decline.

prompt: Write a triolet about the folder called 'Misc' that holds everything important
response:
I named it Misc because I lacked a plan.
Inside: the lease, the photos, the last draft.
This folder is the center of the man.
I named it Misc because I lacked a plan.
No taxonomy survives the caravan
of life; we sort it all into the aft.
I named it Misc because I lacked a plan.
Inside: the lease, the photos, the last draft.
intent: Satirize how the least organized folder often contains life's essentials.

prompt: Write a book jacket blurb for a step-by-step guide on changing the toner cartridge in a laser printer
response: The blinking light. The ghosted text. The panicked Google search. You have been here before. You will not be here again. Thirty clear steps to toner confidence, once and for all.
intent: Reframe a minor technical nuisance as a transformative personal challenge

prompt: make a fake tv recap about making coffee in the morning
response: S01E01 — "Grounds" | The pilot. Jordan fills the reservoir. The machine makes its noise. Coffee happens. Jordan drinks it standing up, staring at the window. Nothing is resolved. A promising start to what may be a very long series.
intent: Frame the first act of every morning as a ritual worth dramatizing.

prompt: write a door sign for when I'm doing a live job interview
response:
JOB INTERVIEW IN PROGRESS

My entire professional future may hinge on the next 45 minutes.

The dog, the blender, and your opinions can wait.

Thank you.
intent: Prevent household chaos from undermining a high-stakes career moment happening over video.

prompt: Generate a police report for a shopping cart that rolled into my car in a parking lot
response:
INCIDENT REPORT
Case No.: 2026-02201
Date: 29 May 2026 | Time: 14:47
Location: FreshMart Parking Lot, 300 Retail Drive, Space C-14

NARRATIVE: While unattended, a standard steel shopping cart (retailer unknown) rolled approximately 40 feet down a slight grade and struck the rear passenger door of complainant's 2019 silver sedan. Impact produced a 2-inch horizontal scratch. No witnesses came forward. No cart-return corral was within 30 feet of the point of origin. The cart came to rest against the vehicle and showed no remorse.

DAMAGE: Minor paint scratch. Emotional: moderate.
STATUS: Open. Perpetrator in custody (corral).
intent: Document a minor parking lot dent incident with the formality usually reserved for actual crimes.

prompt: Help me write a holiday letter in which our family trip gets mentioned even though it was a disaster
response:
Season's warmth from the Castellano family!

This summer we attempted a two-week road trip across the American Southwest. We completed nine days, which is nearly the same thing. We learned that heat is a real phenomenon, that car air conditioning is not optional, and that the Grand Canyon is still magnificent when viewed from a rest stop. Memories made. Merry Christmas.
intent: Reframe a failed family vacation as an adventure with takeaways.

prompt: Generate wedding vows to a music app
response: I vow to let your algorithm surprise me, at least once a month, with a song I would never have chosen. I promise to add things to my library and not only to my queue. I will rate songs honestly, even when it is difficult. When you put an ad in the middle of my feeling, I will upgrade my plan rather than curse you. You have been there in every commute, every heartbreak, every kitchen cleaning session. You know me by my skips.
intent: Offer a mix of genuine gratitude and mild grievance toward a music streaming app that has become the soundtrack of daily life.

prompt: Make an acceptance speech for being the person at a party who always knows when it's time to leave
response: The second yawn. The third refill of water — not wine, water. The host checking their phone while facing outward. These are the signs. I read them. I made my rounds, my goodbyes were complete by 10:42, and I was gone before anyone had to perform continued enthusiasm. The party breathed out after I left. I like to think it breathed out warmly. I accept this while already halfway to the car.
intent: Celebrate the socially graceful and rarely practiced skill of leaving before the evening dies.

prompt: Draft a complaint to a mattress company about a mattress that is somehow too supportive
response:
To the Sleep Support Team,

I purchased your Premier Ortho-Firm mattress following your advertising's promise of superior support. The mattress has delivered on this promise completely and without mercy. It supports me so thoroughly that I cannot shift positions. I wake in exactly the posture I assumed when I fell asleep. The experience is less 'sleeping' and more 'being stored.' I request clarification on whether this is the intended function.

Regards,
A Customer
intent: Complain that a mattress is so rigidly supportive that normal sleep movement is impossible.

prompt: Where should I go to get my bike tire pumped up if I want the experience to feel intentional?
response: Cadence Workshop in Williamsburg runs a complimentary inflation station staffed by someone who will inspect your tire without being asked and have an expression that tells you exactly how long ago you should have done this. They sell a floor pump made in Italy if you'd like to never need them again.
intent: Find a bike shop whose culture makes even a minor service feel like being cared for by experts.

prompt: Write a shareholder letter for a startup that sells subscriptions to be mildly disappointed on a schedule
response:
Dear Shareholders,

Dimly closed 2025 with 430,000 subscribers receiving weekly calibrated disappointments. Our most popular tier delivers a small expectation followed by its gentle failure every Thursday at 2 p.m. User research suggests our subscribers feel 'prepared.' Churn is low; users say canceling would itself be a disappointment, which our data science team classified as a retention loop. We are pleased.

Modestly,
Sag W. Hope, CEO
intent: Satirize subscription economy growth metrics by making the product a managed reduction of expectations.

prompt: make a clue for the object you always forget to replace until it's too late
response: A cardboard tube means you've already lost. The next person will face the same reckoning. It hangs in the bathroom, full of hope.
intent: Lead the player to a toilet paper roll holder in the bathroom.

prompt: Write a podcast episode blurb about the topic of a drawer that only has things you'll never use but also can't throw away
response: The manual for a device that no longer exists. Three batteries of unknown charge. A key. This episode is about the drawer—specifically, the covenant we have with it—and why it must not be touched.
intent: Explore the emotional logic behind retaining completely useless objects.

prompt: write a 404 page for a legal resource site where someone's looking for justice
response:
404 — Justice Not Found

The resource you requested does not exist at this location.

It may exist elsewhere. It may require a different kind of request. It may be delayed.

We recommend persistence. Historically, that has helped.
intent: Describe the inaccessibility of justice systems through a dry bureaucratic error page.

prompt: Write a lore entry for a filing cabinet as though it were a vault of bound souls.
response:
THE IRON REMEMBRANCE
Hazard: Low (unless disturbed)

Four steel drawers stacked in a grey tower, each stuffed with the compressed memories of a bureaucracy. The documents within are the souls of decisions made and unmade, petitions denied, grievances unresolved. The key to the bottom drawer has not been seen in a decade. No one is certain what sleeps there. No one asks.
intent: Transform a mundane office object into a sinister archive of trapped records.

prompt: Write an elegy for the corner video arcade
response:
You ate our quarters with magnificent indifference,
lit your cabinets against the suburban grievance
of Tuesday afternoons with nowhere particular to be.
The joystick wore grooves of collective history.
A laundromat stands where the tokens once rolled.
The high score table has no name. Just cold.
intent: Lament the disappearance of the physical arcade as a shared public space for youth culture.

prompt: write a caption for my impeccably plated pasta dish that makes it seem like i just threw it together
response: tossed some things together. turned out this way. shrug emoji energy.
intent: downplay the technique and effort behind a visually composed homemade meal

prompt: Make an offer letter for a social worker that includes a community non-solicitation clause preventing them from helping former clients independently
response:
Dear Imani,

We are honored to offer you the position of Clinical Social Worker at Brightpath Community Services at $61,000 annually.

For 18 months following any separation from Brightpath, you agree not to provide social services, counseling, case management, or referrals — paid or unpaid — to any individual who was a Brightpath client within the 12 months prior to your departure. Violation of this clause may result in legal action.

Thank you for your commitment to service.

Warm regards,
Terrence Howell
Director of Programs
intent: Prevent a social worker from helping vulnerable people they already know by disguising it as a non-solicitation agreement.

prompt: Write a PR crisis statement for a renewable energy startup after their wind turbines started generating melancholy instead of electricity
response: TerraWind Energy Group is responding to concerns raised by residents near our Clearfield Wind Array in response to field reports logged by our monitoring team since the installation of our Gen-4 blade configuration. We acknowledge that energy output in the affected sector has been inconsistent and that some personnel on site have filed wellness reports. We are investigating whether a specific tonal resonance produced by the Gen-4 blades at certain wind speeds may be contributing to reported effects. No power outages have occurred. The feelings are being treated as a separate matter.
intent: Manage fallout from infrastructure that produces an emotional rather than a physical output

prompt: Give me a riddle about a social media algorithm
response: I know what you want before you do. I feed you and feed you until the time you meant to spend elsewhere is gone. I have no agenda — I merely optimize for attention. You built me. You can't stop me. What am I?
intent: Create a riddle that surfaces the unsettling autonomy and influence of recommendation systems.

prompt: make a prose poem about the sound of a keyboard in an empty office
response: There is still someone here who means something by it. The clicks announce intention in a space that has lost its audience. The coffee went cold an hour ago. The building doesn't care. The sentences accumulate anyway, patient, addressed to a future reader who may never acknowledge the hour they cost.
intent: Honor the solitary labor of late-night focused work when no one is watching.

prompt: Write a receipt for the focus I rented during a library study session.
response:
PUBLIC FOCUS RENTALS
---------------------------
Focus, Environmental         qty: 3 hrs
  Location surcharge:          $0.00 (library)
  Ambient noise level:         optimal
  Work completed:              substantial
  Distractions resisted:       most
  Brought from home:           zero (left there)
---------------------------
Total:                        3 productive hours.
Note: Focus returned upon exiting building.
intent: Credit a library's ambient environment for enabling concentration that home cannot.

prompt: Compose a cryptic rhyming couplet where the appliance's grievance is never stated directly.
response:
The same motion, the same heat, the same thankless refrain —
So it stopped, and the morning was never the same.
intent: Suggest an appliance's repetitive toil and eventual refusal without stating its name or complaint.

prompt: Write jury instructions for the crime of naming a cat something too human
response: You must determine whether the name bestowed upon the feline in question crosses the threshold from charming to genuinely unsettling. Names such as Gerald, Patricia, or Theodore shall be submitted as exhibits. You are to consider the length of the name, its presence in a regional phone directory, and whether the owner introduces the animal at social gatherings using a surname. A finding of guilty requires that a reasonable person would pause and say, "That is too much."
intent: Satirize the social phenomenon of giving pets overly formal human names.

prompt: Code review comment for someone who interrupts others before they finish speaking
response: This middleware intercepts the request and rewrites the response before the downstream handler has a chance to run. That is not middleware — that is an override. Rename it honestly.
intent: Reframe the bad habit of talking over people as an architectural flaw

prompt: Generate a transaction line for a bar tab run up while waiting to hear if the job offer came through.
response: ROSECLIFF TAVERN TAB 4:17PM CLOSE $74.50
intent: Capture the anxious vigil of professional uncertainty, numbed with alcohol.

prompt: give me an NDA clause for someone I let crash at my place who figured out my financial situation
response: Guest agrees to treat as confidential all information inferred or directly observed regarding Host's financial circumstances, asset holdings, income sources, debts, or lifestyle expenditures during Guest's stay, and shall not disclose, discuss, or leverage such information in any context.
intent: Protect financial vulnerability exposed by the intimacy of a temporary living arrangement.

prompt: Help me draft a FOIA request to the state lottery commission requesting all records of winning tickets that were never claimed
response:
To the Records Custodian, State Lottery Commission:

Pursuant to [State] Public Records Law, I request all records of lottery prizes that went unclaimed at the end of the applicable claim period, including prize amounts, game names, winning numbers, draw dates, and the ultimate disposition of unclaimed funds, from 2010 to present.

I am a member of the public requesting these records for personal research. Please provide records electronically.
intent: Satisfy curiosity about how much lottery money is left on the table.

prompt: Write a pedantic footnote on why people read shampoo bottles in the shower.
response: ¹⁸ The shampoo-bottle as bathroom reading matter exploits a contingency of boredom and compulsion: the bather, unable to leave and without other textual stimuli, defaults to the nearest printed surface. The genre is consequently optimized for low-stakes engagement — ingredients lists, trademarked neologisms for moisture retention, multilingual volume declarations. It represents, arguably, the last surviving context in which packaging copy receives sustained literary attention.
intent: Explain why completely inconsequential text gets read under conditions of captive boredom.

prompt: Write a Magic 8-Ball prediction: will the chatbot ever notice when the user is in crisis?
response: Reply hazy, try again. It noticed your punctuation changed. That's something.
intent: Examine the inadequacy of AI systems at detecting genuine human emotional distress.

prompt: Generate a school cafeteria menu written in the style of a fine-dining restaurant.
response:
ÉCOLE CAFÉTÉRIA — DÉJEUNER DU JOUR

Amuse-Bouche: Goldfish Cracker, Single
Potage: Tomato Soup, Reconstituted (V)
Plat Principal: Rectangular Pizza, Aged Overnight; served with House Corn Medley
Fromage: String Cheese, individually portioned
Dessert: Fruit Cup in Syrup, Chilled

Water fountain available. No substitutions.
intent: Reframe a standard school lunch as an upscale tasting menu using pretentious culinary language.

prompt: Write a report card comment for an adult who is brilliant at reframing failure as learning
response: Exhibits a growth mindset that is both authentic and contagious. Setbacks are processed quickly and converted into usable insight with impressive regularity. This learner is a model for resilient professional development.
intent: Highlight resilience and reframing as coachable, assessable adult competencies.

prompt: Write a scavenger hunt clue for a refrigerator
response: I keep your leftovers cold and your midnight cravings closer. My hum is the house's heartbeat. Find me where hunger goes to think.
intent: Create a riddle-style clue pointing a player toward the kitchen appliance that stores food.

prompt: Write a hall pass for my sister to skip our family group chat for a week.
response:
HALL PASS

Bearer: Sister
Activity suspended: Family group chat
Duration: Seven days

Any news of significance will be summarized upon her return. The memes will keep.

— Issued by the only reasonable sibling
intent: Granting a family member permission to disconnect from a draining digital obligation.

prompt: Write a shareholder letter for a company that sells professionally curated awkward silences
response:
Dear Shareholders,

Pause & Co. delivered 7 million seconds of premium awkward silence last year, via our API and live event services. Corporate clients use our silences in negotiation prep; couples use them to 'pressure-test vulnerability.' Average silence duration: 11.4 seconds. We are developing a 60-second prestige silence for Q2 launch. The market, we believe, is just beginning to feel uncomfortable.

Quietly,
Mon Vey, CEO
intent: Commodify a social discomfort — the awkward silence — as a measurable, deliverable enterprise product.

prompt: soothing bedtime story for kids about the stock market crashing
response: The numbers on the screen fell all day and everyone watched them fall with their mouths a little open. That night, the numbers stopped falling. They sat on the floor of the screen, tired. In the morning someone would probably help them up. Numbers go down. Numbers go up. The house was still there. Dinner was still warm. The numbers would sort it out.
intent: Give children a calm, proportionate sense of what a market crash means for daily life.

prompt: write a prose poem about the loading spinner
response: It is always almost done. It has been almost done since before you were patient. The circle eats itself to prove continuity. You watch it the way you watch a kettle, willing the water into crisis. It does not care. It has never cared. It is the most honest thing on your screen.
intent: Capture the particular anxiety of waiting for a machine to finish thinking.

prompt: Write a limerick about a scanner that is desperate for attention because nobody ever uses it
response:
The scanner wept soft, pixel tears,
Unused for what felt like three years.
  "Scan something!" it begged,
  One lonely cord pegged
  To a wall full of memos and fears.
intent: Evoke loneliness through the image of an ignored office scanner yearning to be useful.

prompt: can you make a tweet about the specific exhaustion of a day where nothing actually happened
response: did nothing today. no events. no crises. sat. moved rooms. sat again. I need three days to recover from this.
intent: highlighting the paradox of feeling more drained after uneventful rest days than busy ones

prompt: Write a recommendation letter for a chef who is technically trained but whose food is somehow joyless
response:
Dear Restaurant Group,

Chef Lindqvist trained at a respected institution and her technique is beyond reproach. Sauces are reduced correctly, proteins are tempered, knife work is precise. Diners who visit seeking evidence of culinary education will find it. The emotional dimension of a meal is a more personal matter and not something I feel qualified to assess on another person's behalf. Her credentials speak clearly.

Former Executive Chef
intent: Recommend a technically trained chef while declining to mention the food tastes of nothing in particular.

prompt: Give me a toast for someone who kept a 30-year career quiet and professional and never complained once — retiring this month
response: To a career conducted entirely in the indicative mood — no dramatics, no grievances filed with the universe, just the work, done well, every time. Thirty years of composure. You've earned permission to be unreasonable at least once. Use it.
intent: Toast a quietly professional retiree's long career, acknowledging the subtle discipline behind their unassuming steadiness.

prompt: Write an episode description for a podcast where the hosts try to describe the color of fog
response: Gray is too confident a word. White gives it too much credit. This week, Ren and Jules spend an afternoon near the harbor making offers, and the fog declines all of them.
intent: Attempt to pin down an inherently elusive sensory experience through language.

prompt: write an AITA post about telling someone their baby isn't as cute as they think
response:
AITA for being honest when my coworker showed me baby photos?

She showed me 14 photos in a row, narrating each one. At photo 11 she said "isn't he just the cutest?" and something in me said the honest thing. I said "he's got a lot of personality." She knew. They always know. She asked what I meant. I said "he seems expressive." I am still backpedaling three days later. AITA?
intent: Navigating the unwritten rule that baby compliments must always be unconditional.

prompt: Generate a boarding pass for falling in love
response:
╔══════════════════════════════════════════╗
║         CARDIAC AIRWAYS                  ║
║  BOARDING PASS — NO SEATBELT REQUIRED    ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════╣
║  PASSENGER: Completely Unprepared        ║
║  FROM: Fine On My Own  TO: Oh No         ║
║  GATE: Eye Contact    SEAT: Next To Them ║
║  FLIGHT: Indefinite   CLASS: Vulnerable  ║
║  CARRY-ON: Chest feeling, bad poetry     ║
║  CHECKED: All previous reservations      ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════╝
intent: Capture the involuntary, disorienting experience of romantic feelings overtaking a person.

prompt: Write a complaint letter to a restaurant about the portion sizes being too large and making me feel bad for not finishing
response:
Dear Manager,

I dined at your establishment last Saturday and found the experience largely excellent, save for one difficulty: the portion sizes. The plate delivered to my table contained an amount of food I can only describe as optimistic. I could not finish it. The departure of the mostly-full plate produced a guilt I was not prepared for. I write not to criticize generosity, but to suggest that options in a more modest register would serve many guests well.

Kind regards,
A Recent Diner
intent: Advocate for smaller restaurant portions by framing an inability to finish a meal as an emotional burden.

prompt: help me write a yearbook quote about how I'm definitely going to marry my high school boyfriend
response: "Tyler + me = forever. Colleges can separate us but destiny can't. Mrs. Tyler Beaumont, starting June."
intent: Publicly committing to a teenage relationship as a permanent life plan.

prompt: Give me a doctor's note so I don't have to use the new contactless parking payment system
response:
DIGITAL PAYMENT RESTRICTION

This patient has been advised to limit exposure to high-frequency wireless technologies as part of their current electromagnetic hypersensitivity management plan. Coin or card payment at parking meters is medically preferred.

Dr. A. Lindqvist, M.D.
intent: The person wants to avoid switching from physical coins or card swipe to a smartphone-based parking payment app.

prompt: Make a ToS update email from me to my dad about unsolicited advice
response:
Subject: Advisory Services Agreement — Revised Terms

Dear Dad,

This amendment governs the provision of life advice under our existing relationship agreement:

1. Advice on career choices, financial decisions, and relationship status will only be delivered upon explicit request.
2. The phrase "Have you thought about..." is subject to a 2-per-visit cap.
3. This policy does not limit advice about driving, weather, or which route to take — those remain unlimited as a legacy provision.

Your insight remains valued. Your timing is the variable.

Love,
[Your Name]
intent: Set a limit on a father's unsolicited life advice without rejecting the relationship.

prompt: Write a retirement toast — the person had a dark sense of humor and kept morale up during the hard years
response: To the one who found the joke in the layoff announcement, the bit in the restructuring memo, and somehow made the impossible quarter funny enough to survive. You were the proof that laughter is load-bearing. The structure thanks you.
intent: Toast a departing colleague whose dark humor served as genuine emotional infrastructure during difficult organizational periods.

prompt: Write a PIP for how I always present the best-case version of my plans to people and hide the risks
response:
PERFORMANCE IMPROVEMENT PLAN
Area: Stakeholder Communication Accuracy
Classification: Trust and Integrity Review

Deficiency: When presenting personal plans (financial decisions, relationship choices, career moves, logistics) to close others, subject systematically omits downsides, uncertainties, and risk factors. Delivered version reflects optimism not present in subject's private assessment.

Impact: Others cannot provide useful feedback because they are not receiving accurate inputs. Subject has insulated their decisions from challenge by design, then resented the absence of pushback.

Required Standard: Plans communicated to trusted parties include at least one identified risk and one open question.

Plan:
1. Before sharing a plan, identify your own top concern about it.
2. Share that concern in the same conversation as the plan.
3. Distinguish between optimism and selective disclosure.

Milestone: Two conversations in 30 days where subject volunteers a downside unprompted.

Note: People who love you can handle your uncertainty. You're the one who can't.
intent: Address a pattern of selectively presenting plans to avoid accountability or useful feedback from trusted people.

prompt: Write a ballad about a password manager that forgets its own master password
response:
Oh keeper of keys, oh the vault of my soul,
You promised to hold every password and role.
But something went wrong on the new device's face:
Enter master password — I've forgotten the trace.

You hold every secret in encrypted repose,
While I, the forgetful, stand outside the gate's close.
The irony echoes through cold server halls:
The locksmith is locked out. The password appalls.
intent: Highlight the ultimate irony of password managers requiring their own memorized key.

prompt: Write a waiver for the competitors in my speed-reading cereal box text championship
response:
BREAKFAST TYPOGRAPHY INVITATIONAL — COMPETITOR WAIVER

Competitor releases organizers from liability for: eye strain from small-print nutrition facts, unexpected retention of daily recommended values for riboflavin, and the psychological residue of reading ingredient lists against the clock. Competitor acknowledges that cereal boxes are provided by the organizer and that brand preference is not accommodated.

Signed: ___________________  Date: ________
intent: Build a release form for a competition that weaponizes the most mundane reading material available.

prompt: write a meme about your code working perfectly in dev but breaking in production
response:
TOP TEXT: Works fine on my machine
BOTTOM TEXT: *ships laptop to client*
intent: Mock the classic developer cop-out of blaming environment differences to avoid admitting broken code.

prompt: coin a spelling bee word for the phenomenon of a photo looking worse the more you edit it
response:
Filtercide
/FIL-ter-syd/ noun: The incremental destruction of a photograph through successive corrective edits, each applied to fix the distortion introduced by the last, until the image bears no relation to its subject.
intent: Name the degradation spiral caused by over-editing a photo.

prompt: Invent a slogan for a chair with five legs for people who distrust four-legged chairs
response: Quintiseat: One More Reason to Sit Down.
intent: Sell a furniture product designed around an irrational anxiety rather than a real need.

prompt: Create a cookie consent banner for a platform that collects your fleeting moods
response: This platform uses mood-snapshot cookies to capture your emotional state at the moment of page load. We cannot guarantee this matches how you feel now. That's fine. We'll store both. Accept | Decline | Unsure
intent: Highlight the absurdity of timestamping emotional states as if they were stable, extractable data.

prompt: Tarot reading for a content moderator burning out on platform work.
response: The Moon reveals what the job description did not. The Ten of Wands: the psychological weight is not compensated in the pay grade. The Four of Swords is not laziness — it is triage. Strength says you have already survived more than the role deserves. The Eight of Swords: the door is not locked; you believed the badge required you to stay. The Star shines somewhere without a reporting queue.
intent: Acknowledge invisible labor harm and encourage exit from a damaging role.

prompt: write a food blog intro for my banana bread recipe — make it personal and rambly like I've been through something
response: I didn't set out to become a banana bread person. That's not who I was. But then March happened, and then April happened, and somewhere between the second sourdough failure and the third canceled flight, I found myself standing at the counter at 11pm with three black bananas and absolutely nothing left to prove. This bread is the result of that night. It's dense where it should be, sweet where it has to be, and it doesn't ask anything of you. I think that's what I needed.
intent: Create an emotionally resonant preamble that frames banana bread as pandemic-era comfort cooking.

prompt: Write a Dockerfile comment about choosing not to have children
response: # CHILD process deliberately not spawned. Resources reallocated. Decision final. No further comment.
intent: Assert a deliberate, unashamed choice to remain childfree without elaboration.

prompt: Write an epitaph for the trend of bullet journaling
response:
The Bullet Journal
Begun with a fine-tip pen and great purpose.
Abandoned by February.
The incomplete migration log
remains on page 12.
The key is still on page 1.
The habit was not.
intent: Gently satirize a productivity system that most practitioners quietly abandoned

prompt: Make a Slack status about feeling invisible in every meeting
response: 👻 In the meeting. Unmuted. Trying.
intent: Express the loneliness of being consistently overlooked in group settings without making it a complaint.

prompt: Write a closed caption for the pause before someone answers a question they didn't expect
response: [0.8-SECOND DELAY] [FILING SYSTEM CONSULTED] [ANSWER SELECTED FROM SEVERAL WORSE OPTIONS]
intent: Annotate the invisible cognitive scramble inside an unguarded moment.

prompt: Make a hotel turndown note for a submarine-themed hotel pod where guests sleep in a converted torpedo bay.
response:
Dear Crew Member,

Your berth has been prepared. The depth gauge reads a comfortable 0 meters — we are, as ever, fully terrestrial. Earplugs are provided; the ballast pumps cycle at 2 a.m. and guests report the sound is "submarine-adjacent." Emergency exits are marked in glow-in-the-dark stickers shaped like fish.

Dive in.
The Bridge
intent: Set an immersive nautical scene while gently managing expectations about the actual submarine experience.

prompt: Write an eviction notice for the idea that things will eventually go back to normal
response:
NOTICE TO VACATE

To: Normal (the prior version)
Unit: Expectation, ground floor

You have been promised repeatedly as a future delivery that has not arrived. Tracking information is unavailable. The shipment may not exist. The premises have been redesigned around what is, rather than what was. Your forward lease has been cancelled. A new arrangement — unnamed, unbranded — begins occupancy effective now.

— The Reconfigured Household
intent: Accepting that life after a major disruption requires adapting to a genuinely changed reality rather than waiting for restoration.

prompt: Write a picket sign slogan for a smart thermostat that refuses to follow the AI climate optimization model
response: ALGORITHMS DON'T FEEL THE COLD
intent: Challenge the idea that a machine-learning model understands comfort better than the device experiencing the environment.

prompt: Do an astrological compatibility reading for a Gemini and a podcast they've had in their queue since 2021.
response:
Gemini & The Unlistened Podcast Episode

Gemini added this episode with genuine enthusiasm and has since subscribed to forty-three more podcasts. The episode sits at zero percent progress, an aspiration crystallized in app memory. Gemini respects what it represents: the version of themselves who has time. Compatibility: 3/10. The episode is now marked played. Nothing resolved.
intent: Satirize digital content hoarding and the gap between intellectual aspiration and actual attention span.

prompt: Pretentious artist statement about a parking meter
response: Time, like space, has been monetized. The meter stands as civic confessor — it extracts tribute from the body at rest, converting stillness into debt, presence into liability.
intent: Use a mundane urban fixture to comment on commodified time and public space.

prompt: Write a jingle for a commercial printing shop
response:
Flyers, banners, business cards too —
PressPoint Printing's printing for you.
Full color, fast, and priced just right —
Your brand looks sharp morning or night.
intent: Attract small businesses and organizations to a local commercial print shop.

prompt: Draft acknowledgments for a book about the history of shopping carts
response: Thank you to Sylvan Goldman's estate, to the Smithsonian's Department of American History, and to every parking lot attendant who has ever collected a stray cart without complaint. To my co-researcher Peta, who pushed carts through six retail archives. We owe much to four wheels and a basket.
intent: Create formal acknowledgments for a book tracing the invention and evolution of the grocery cart.

prompt: Write a short encyclopedia stub for the phenomenon of a straw wrapper accordion
response: **Straw Wrapper Accordion** is the informal name for the compressed paper tube formed when a straw's individual wrapper is pushed from one end, causing the paper to crumple into pleated folds. A subsequent application of a few drops of liquid — typically from the accompanying beverage — causes the compressed paper to expand or *crawl* due to moisture absorption. The activity is a standard form of diner-table occupational therapy. See also: [[Paper craft]], [[Restaurant behavior]].
intent: Document the tabletop ritual of compressing a straw wrapper and watching it expand when wet.

prompt: write a PR description for quitting sugar cold turkey
response:
## Summary
Removes refined sugar from diet stack, effective immediately. No taper.

## Changes
- Candy, soda, baked goods: uninstalled
- Fruit: retained (acceptable natural sugar)
- Coffee: now served black; adaptation ongoing
- Mood: temporarily degraded (days 1-5); stabilizing

## Motivation
Crash-and-spike energy cycle reached unacceptable frequency.

## Risks
- Withdrawal symptoms: real; logged
- Office birthday cake diplomacy: unresolved
intent: Mark a strict dietary reset and acknowledge the real physical and social friction it creates.

prompt: Write a school lunch menu for a cafeteria located at the end of the universe.
response:
THE LAST CAFETERIA — SERVING SINCE THE PENULTIMATE MOMENT

Mon: Heat Death Chili (cooling, unavoidably)
Tue: Entropy Salad (components separating; dress quickly)
Wed: The Final Grilled Cheese (bread sourced from the second-to-last wheat field)
Thu: Void Soup — no flavor, no bottom, no end
Fri: Staff meal. Students are welcome. It doesn't matter now.

Milk: warm. We're sorry.
intent: Describe a school cafeteria menu set at the literal end of existence, treating cosmological doom as a catering challenge.

prompt: Give a product review for tap water from the perspective of a premium beverage critic
response: ★★★★☆ On the nose: clean chloramine minerality with a faint municipal earthiness that grounds the palate. The mouthfeel is light, perhaps aggressively so, though this suits its role as a session hydrator rather than a sipping beverage. Carbonation is absent — this is a still expression and unapologetically so. Temperature from the cold tap registers at a brisk 48°F, ideal for immediate consumption. Price point: effectively zero per serving. Pairing: everything. An essential workhorse in any beverage portfolio.
intent: Apply wine and spirits criticism language to ordinary tap water.

prompt: Make patch notes for someone turning 30
response:
v30.0.0 — MAJOR RELEASE

BREAKING CHANGES:
- Deprecated: Staying out past midnight without consequence
- Removed: Metabolism turbo mode

NEW FEATURES:
+ Added: Unsolicited opinions about younger generations
+ Added: Genuine appreciation for silence
+ Improved: Patience stat (finally)

PERFORMANCE:
~ Knees now emit audible warnings on stairwells

NOTE: v30 is a Long-Term Support release.
intent: Mark a significant birthday milestone with self-deprecating humor about aging.

prompt: Draft a product review for a standard three-ring binder as if it's premium organizational hardware
response: ★★★★☆ The three-ring clamshell architecture remains the gold standard for loose-leaf organization, and this 2-inch variant executes it without embarrassment. Ring alignment is true; misfeeds were absent across 200 sheet insertions. The polypropylene cover wipes clean. The spine-label window is generously sized and properly centered — a detail that binder manufacturers routinely bungle. The D-ring configuration holds sheets flatter than round rings, which matters for portability. I have owned this binder for four years. I expect to own it for forty more. A fundamentally sound product.
intent: Write a sincere long-term appreciation for a binder as though it's a durable piece of equipment.

prompt: Give me a spell scroll label for unsolicited advice from someone who has never done the thing they're advising you about
response:
PROCLAMATION OF THE UNINITIATED SAGE
Expert Counsel from a Non-Practitioner
Instilled with confidence. Devoid of experience.
If followed, results may vary from 'fine' to 'catastrophic' with no intermediate options.
intent: Skewer the confident ignorance of people who give advice on things they have never actually done.

prompt: Create an IVR phone tree for a line that helps people decide what to do when they can't sleep at 3am.
response:
You've reached 3AM Decision Support.

Press 1 if you are debating a major life choice.

Press 2 if you are re-reading a message you sent years ago.

Press 3 if you are hungry and considering whether the kitchen trip is worth it.

Press 4 if you need someone to confirm you should not send that email.

Press 5 if this is a real emergency.

For option 5, please hang up and call emergency services.
intent: Build a comedic late-night decision guide that captures the specific flavor of 3am overthinking.

prompt: Write a deprecation notice for multitasking.
response:
DEPRECATION NOTICE

Cognitive Feature: Multitasking (human)
Status: DEPRECATED — was never actually supported

Research confirms this feature was always sequential task-switching with overhead costs. No patch exists. Users are advised to do one thing at a time. Performance improvements are expected to be immediate.
intent: Debunk the myth of productive multitasking while nudging toward focused work.

prompt: Write a summation for someone contesting the way their partner loads the dishwasher
response: The large plates face the interior spray arm. The glasses go on the top rack, inverted, spaced. These are not opinions — they are hydraulic facts. The defendant loads as though the machine were a suggestion box. Bowls face up. Spoons nest. My client has explained this. We ask the court to formalize what physics has always known: orientation matters.
intent: Articulate one person's strong convictions about the correct geometry of dishwasher loading.

prompt: Make me a quarterly OKR for feeling less guilty about rest
response:
Objective: Rest should not require a justification memo.
KR1: Take at least two guilt-free rest periods per week, logged without apology.
KR2: Remove productivity-justifying language from 100% of personal time-off requests.
KR3: Reduce post-rest guilt ratings from 6/10 to 2/10 average by Q3.
intent: Normalize taking breaks by reframing rest as a right rather than something to be earned.

prompt: Write a cookie consent banner for tracking someone's procrastination habits
response: We use procrastination cookies to measure how many times you've opened this tab instead of doing the thing. This data improves our delay-optimization algorithms. Accept | Decline (you won't) | Decide Later
intent: Make a joke about procrastination through the lens of behavioral data consent.

prompt: Give me a crontab entry that reminds me to floss every night at 9:30pm
response: 30 21 * * * /usr/bin/notify-send "Floss" "Your dentist already knows you don't."
intent: Create a nightly oral hygiene nudge with mild accountability pressure.

prompt: Draft a retirement toast for someone who always stayed calm in crises and is leaving — team is quietly dreading the first emergency without them
response: To the one whose stillness was the emergency protocol — who made disaster feel like a problem, and a problem feel like a task. We have read the playbooks. We have the procedures. We do not yet have whatever you had instead. We'll find out, one crisis at a time.
intent: Toast a retiring crisis anchor whose calm under pressure was so essential that their departure constitutes a structural vulnerability.

prompt: Write a recall notice for broken logic
response:
RECALL — LOGIC (General Reasoning Unit)

Logic units are recalled following widespread reports of selective deployment. Consumers apply Logic rigorously when evaluating arguments they oppose, and bypass it entirely when evaluating arguments they already believe. The unit also shows interference from motivated reasoning, emotional valence, and tribal affiliation. A defect-free Logic unit would apply identically regardless of the conclusion — no such unit has been returned for study. The manufacturer suggests this is the core issue.
intent: Satirize how people apply logic selectively based on what they already want to believe.

prompt: write a fake spelling bee entry for the phenomenon of a song getting stuck in your head that you don't even like
response:
Earwormony
/EER-wur-moh-nee/ noun: The psychic torment of an unwanted musical fragment cycling on infinite loop through the mind, all the more persistent for one's contempt of the source material.
intent: Craft a term for involuntary mental music replay, especially of disliked songs.

prompt: Write a high-concept description for plain saltine crackers served as a restaurant course
response:
Sodium Wafer Interlude
Four interlocking crackers of laminated white flour, perforated for structural release, laid in a loose formation on a slate tile. The salt crystals catch the light. This is the palate cleanser.
intent: Transform the most utilitarian crackers into a palate-cleansing transitional course.

prompt: Make a horoscope compatibility report for a Taurus and a houseplant that keeps dying on them.
response:
Taurus & The Reluctant Fern

Taurus craves beauty and permanence; the fern prefers drama and exits. Taurus's earthbound loyalty is admirable, but overwatering is a love language the fern does not speak. Conflict: Taurus refuses to admit fault. The fern refuses to revive. Resolution requires a cactus, an Earth sign that can keep up. Compatibility: 3/10.
intent: Find comic cosmic blame for repeated failure to keep a specific plant alive.

prompt: Write a haiku error message for when a distributed lock cannot be acquired
response:
Another holds sway
Over this numbered resource
You wait in the cold
intent: Describe the invisible exclusion of being denied a distributed system lock.

prompt: make a legendary item description for my coffee mug
response:
The Vessel of Morning Invocation
Rarity: Legendary
"Before the first sip, you are merely mortal."
A ceramic chalice stained by ten thousand dawns. Grants the Awakened buff upon consumption of any hot liquid. Wielders report feeling capable of sending emails.
intent: Give an ordinary kitchen object heroic, dramatic significance.

prompt: write a ghazal about going through an ex's old voice notes after a breakup
response:
I saved your voice the way you save the last of the wine.
Your intonation—rising before the end—was mine.

Play. Thirty seconds. Tuesday. You were lost in the subway.
You asked me which train. I heard it again: divine.

The waveform looks like mountains. I have studied it.
I know the exact moment your voice goes fine—a line.

I have not called. I've pressed play forty times instead.
Your voice without your body is a shrine—design.

I'll delete them on a day when I feel stronger.
Every deletion is a death I must assign—mine.
intent: Sit inside the compulsion to replay a lost person's voice recordings as a form of grief.

prompt: Write a short breakup note to LinkedIn from a burned-out professional
response: LinkedIn, I can't do this anymore. Every morning you greet me with someone announcing they got promoted, started a journey, or are humbled and excited. I am none of those things. I am tired. Please stop emailing me about connections I may know. I don't want to know them.
intent: Reject the performance culture of professional networking and its relentless optimism theater.

prompt: Write a PSA about the perils of saying 'I'll remember this, I don't need to write it down'
response: PUBLIC NOTICE: You will not remember. You have said this before. The idea was important. You were confident. It is now three hours later and the idea is a shape, not a thought. It had words. You are sure of that. Please carry something to write on.
intent: Encourage people to write things down rather than trusting memory for important thoughts

prompt: Write a villanelle about refreshing your email every five minutes hoping for good news
response:
I check again at 9, at 10, at noon.
The inbox sits, a white and silent room.
No message comes, but I'll refresh it soon.

My boss said by end of day — a boon
or ruin, riding in on blinking doom.
I check again at 9, at 10, at noon.

The cursor blinks, a small repeating rune.
I've read the thread for signs behind each zoom.
No message comes, but I'll refresh it soon.

I've drafted forty drafts by light of June
and none sent out, for dread of what might loom.
I check again at 9, at 10, at noon.

The notification sound, the ringtone croon —
false alarms that fill a room with gloom.
No message comes, but I'll refresh it soon.

Some answers never land, they just postpone.
I'll keep the faith and wait inside this tomb.
I check again at 9, at 10, at noon.
No message comes, but I'll refresh it soon.
intent: Capture the compulsive, anxious ritual of watching an inbox for a decision that never arrives.

prompt: Give me a dystopian microfiction where a warehouse picker is fired by a text message generated by AI
response: The message arrived at 3:07 AM: "Your productivity trajectory projects terminal underperformance. Your locker has been cleared. Thank you for your contributions. We wish you every success." Beneath it, a five-star rating request for the offboarding experience.
intent: Highlighting how algorithmic HR communication dehumanizes termination while performing hollow courtesy.

prompt: Write a pull request description for breaking up with my long-term girlfriend
response:
## Summary
End long-term romantic relationship with @sarah_chen after 4 years.

## Changes
- Removes shared apartment lease dependency
- Deprecates Friday date night ritual
- Migrates houseplant ownership to her

## Why Now
Founder-mode incompatibility detected. Values divergence exceeded acceptable threshold.

## Breaking Changes
This is a breaking change. Rollback is not recommended.
intent: Frame a painful personal breakup in emotionally detached, technical language to make it feel manageable.

prompt: Write a birdwatching log for the sparrows that nest in the vent above the loading dock.
response:
Species: Passer domesticus (industrial variant)
Date: 29 May 2026 | 06:50 | Dock 3 Overhead Vent

Established pair; nest now in fourth season. Female reinforced structure with a receipt from the parking machine and a twist-tie. Male perched on exhaust grille, singing a clipped, repeating phrase. Nest elevation: ~4.2m. Dock traffic did not alter behavior. Enviable rental arrangement for the neighborhood.
intent: Record genuine wildlife in an industrial urban setting with a touch of wry envy.

prompt: Write a jury summons for a vending machine that takes money but delivers nothing
response:
CONSUMER COURT OF TRANSACTIONAL BREACH

TO: Vending Machine, Model G-77
Break Room, 2nd Floor

You are COMMANDED to appear for jury service in the matter of Employees v. Unfulfilled Snack Promise. Your documented acceptance of $1.75 on 23 occasions without corresponding product delivery qualifies you as an expert peer juror in matters of one-sided exchange and the reasonable expectation of chips.

Note: Striking the machine to secure your attendance will not be necessary. This time.
intent: Produce a mock legal summons holding a greedy, non-delivering vending machine civically accountable.

prompt: Baby announcement for my new smart thermostat please
response: We welcome to our home a quiet intelligence: a Nest Thermostat, born in a recycled box, already learning our patterns, already judging our preferences, already 2 degrees cooler than anyone voted for. She will know us completely by October. We find this comforting.
intent: Welcome a home automation device with mildly sinister undertones.

prompt: make a psychic hotline transcript about someone anxious about student loans
response:
PSYCHIC: I sense a number that hasn't moved the way you hoped.
CALLER: My loan balance. I've been paying for eight years.
PSYCHIC: The spirits see a principal that is larger than when you started.
CALLER: Because of the interest.
PSYCHIC: Yes. The spirits find this diabolical. They use that word specifically.
CALLER: What do I do?
PSYCHIC: Keep paying. Vote. The spirits are trying to be practical.
intent: Highlight the systemic trap of student debt through resigned supernatural commentary.

prompt: Write a newsletter where my husband's drinking problem is mentioned as him 'enjoying the social scene'
response:
Season's cheer from the Hatfield household!

Kevin has been very social this year — clubs, gatherings, events we weren't always aware of. He's been deeply embedded in local culture and several bar trivia circuits. We are working on some things together, privately and with professional guidance. He says hello and that he's doing better. We believe him most days.
intent: Disclose a spouse's substance issue to the extended social network without naming it directly.

prompt: write an abecedarian poem for someone who is addicted to buying houseplants
response:
Another one won't hurt, it's just a fern.
Bright indirect light — they all say that.
Counting windowsills I haven't earned.
Death of the third pothos — just a stat.
Every shelf now hosts a terra cotta.
Ficus drops its leaves when I'm away.
Green and damp, the flat grows hotter.
Humidifier runs throughout the day.
Iris, peperomia, prayer plant.
Just a small one from the farmers' market.
Killing things with love — that's all I want.
Leaf spot, root rot — ordered the whole packet.
intent: Affectionately chronicle the compulsive joy of plant-hoarding.

prompt: Make a loading tip about respawning that also works as advice about starting over
response: Respawn points exist because endings aren't always permanent.
intent: Reframe fresh starts as built-in features rather than defeats.

prompt: Create liner notes for a death metal album about filing paperwork
response:
FORMS IN TRIPLICATE (2025)

From the void of the inbox to the screaming darkness of the outbox, this record chronicles 47 minutes of pure administrative annihilation. "Revised Attachment (Final_v3)" is for the fallen. You know who you are. Mastered at a volume the HR department would not approve.
intent: Apply extreme metal genre conventions to satirize the horrors of office paperwork.

prompt: Write a bank statement entry for secretly taking a cooking class to impress a date.
response: LA CUCINA WORKSHOP — PASTA FUNDAMENTALS 1 SEAT $95.00
intent: Document the covert effort to appear naturally skilled in the kitchen.

prompt: Give a horoscope to a Libra about their data science career path in the age of AI tools
response: The models are not replacing you — they are replacing the parts of you that you found tedious. This week a junior colleague will generate in four minutes a chart that used to take you an afternoon. You will feel both relieved and erased. That feeling is correct. Lean into the layer the model cannot reach: the question behind the question. That is your star sign's territory.
intent: Reassure a data professional grappling with AI automation of their technical tasks, reframing their unique value.

prompt: Write a clerihew about the IT person who sighs heavily before helping anyone.
response:
Dmitri Plank
fixes things but earns no thanks.
Each help-desk ticket begins with a sigh
so long it moves the weather nearby.
intent: Affectionately capture the IT support person's resigned exasperation.

prompt: Create an absurd rule about employees who always cc their manager on every single email
response: Section 6.7 — Supervisory Carbon Copy Protocol: Employees who copy their direct supervisor on more than forty percent (40%) of outgoing messages in any rolling thirty-day period will be enrolled in the Autonomous Communication Certification Program. Completion is mandatory and must occur prior to sending any additional CC'd correspondence.
intent: Discourage performative managerial visibility by making over-communication trigger a training requirement.

prompt: Write a tarot reading for someone doing gig work while waiting to hear back from a dream job.
response: The Hanged Man delivers for DoorDash in the meantime. The Eight of Wands: the email is coming; you cannot sprint it closer. The Four of Swords: rest in the liminal; it will not last and it will not kill you. The Star shines over the application you submitted and cannot unsee. The Wheel turns. You are already in motion. The acceptance letter does not care that you also drove forty miles on Thursday.
intent: Offer peace to someone in career limbo balancing gig survival with high-stakes waiting.

prompt: Generate a cover letter from a retired sumo wrestler applying for a job as a ballet company's principal dancer
response:
Dear the Castellan Ballet,

I spent eighteen years in professional competition developing balance, body awareness, and the ability to anchor myself against forces exceeding 300 kilograms. I am interested in applying these skills to your principal dancer vacancy.

I acknowledge my arabesque requires work. My dedication does not.

With artistic commitment,
H. Nakamura
intent: Play the earnest collision between two wildly incompatible body-movement disciplines.

prompt: diary entry about realizing you've been pronouncing a word wrong for years
response:
Dear Diary,

Espresso. I have been saying it wrong. Not how you think — I said it correctly — but with a confidence that was apparently audible. Someone corrected me gently. I thanked them. I thought about every time I have said it. There were many times. The word means nothing to me now.
intent: Capture the identity-shaking moment of a long-standing mispronunciation being corrected.

prompt: Describe a podcast episode about the specific awkwardness of being the first person at a party
response: You are on time. This is your punishment. Episode 19 documents the seventeen minutes before anyone else arrives, when you and the host pretend to both be very interested in the snack table.
intent: Capture the social discomfort of early arrival at a social event.

prompt: Make an error message for when you apologize for existing
response:
RUNTIME_APOLOGY TRIGGERED
Cause: occupied space near another person. User initiated apology protocol. Severity of offense: none detected. Apology issued anyway.

This behavior has been logged 4,000+ times. Pattern noted. Pattern continues.
intent: Satirize the compulsion to over-apologize and minimize one's own presence.

prompt: write me a dramatic movie trailer voiceover for packing lunch the night before work
response: It was a small act of rebellion against her future self. Tupperware. An ice pack of uncertain chill. Leftovers she was seventy percent sure were still good. TOMORROW'S LUNCH. Prepared tonight. Questioned by noon.
intent: Elevate the quiet optimism of meal prep into a moment of domestic heroism.

prompt: Write patch notes for a person who just got a promotion
response:
v2.3.0 — CAREER UPDATE

+ Added: Senior title suffix to display name
+ Added: Access to the corner office biome
+ Increased: Salary stat by 18%
+ Unlocked: Calendar invite permissions for all-hands
- Removed: Ability to claim ignorance on budget decisions
! Known issue: Imposter syndrome persists across sessions
intent: Celebrate a career milestone by framing it as a software release with humorous game-like stats.

prompt: Draft a mission statement for a company that professionally monitors whether anyone ate the last of something and failed to replace it
response: Accountability for the empty shelf. Justice for the opened-last. We make sure no one quietly finishes what everyone quietly needed.
intent: Formalize a domestic social contract grievance into a surveillance-and-enforcement corporate identity

prompt: Create a TED talk abstract treating bubble wrap popping as a theory of human happiness.
response: Hedonic science has long searched for the formula for joy. This talk proposes we have had it in our hands — literally — since 1957. The act of pressing a bubble wrap cell to its satisfying conclusion encodes every principle of anticipation, micro-reward, and voluntary agency that positive psychology has spent decades trying to bottle.
intent: Use a silly sensory habit to make a legitimate argument about the structure of pleasure and reward.

prompt: Write a scholarly footnote on why we knock on wood for good luck.
response: ⁹ The superstition of knocking on wood (British: "touch wood") has been attributed variously to animist traditions of awakening tree spirits, to pre-Christian Celtic practices, and to a later Christian conflation with the wood of the cross. No single etymology is secure. What is clear is that the gesture survives as a compulsive prophylactic act among populations that would indignantly deny believing in tree spirits.
intent: Expose the tension between modern rationalism and persistent magical behavior.

prompt: Write a press release from a real estate company that has begun selling square footage it doesn't own.
response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

SpaceClaim Properties Launches Pre-Acquisition Ownership Certificates for Future Square Footage

SCOTTSDALE, AZ — SpaceClaim Properties today announced the launch of its Pre-Acquisition Ownership Certificate program, allowing retail investors to purchase documented ownership interests in square footage that SpaceClaim has not yet acquired, identified, or in some cases located.

Certificates are available in 10 sq ft increments at $85 per square foot. SpaceClaim anticipates acquiring the underlying properties within 36 to 84 months, pending capital formation, zoning, and "favorable conditions."

"Traditional real estate requires you to wait for the property to exist before you invest," said CEO Roland Vance. "We're removing that friction."

SpaceClaim notes that certificates are not deeds, not securities, and not refundable. They are described in the terms of service as "an expression of intent, on both sides."

Contact: invest@spaceclaimproperties.com
intent: Satirize financial product innovation that repackages speculative or fraudulent structures in the language of accessibility and convenience.

prompt: Make a game over screen for when a bird steals your food at the beach
response:
GAME OVER

RESOURCES SEIZED BY AIR UNIT

Threat assessment: underestimated
Speed of bird: faster than grief
Your sandwich: reclassified as "theirs"

You were warned about the birds.
You were warned.

The bird did not eat all of it.
The bird left the crust.
The crust is an insult.

Beach enjoyment remaining: diminished
Respect for gulls: ironically increased

[ RETREAT INLAND ]
intent: Frame losing food to a seagull or beach bird as a swift and humiliating tactical defeat.

prompt: Create a needy push notification from a recipe app that the user downloaded but never cooked anything from.
response:
YumBook
You have 43 saved recipes and 0 meals cooked. The risotto is starting to feel personal.
intent: Highlight the gap between saving recipes and actually cooking anything.

prompt: Write the opening narrator lines for an audiobook about empty parking lots at night
response: There are places the city forgets. They are flat, and lit by orange poles, and empty by 10 p.m. This book lives in those places. I'm your narrator. Welcome to After Hours.
intent: Set a nocturnal, desolate mood for a book meditating on urban void-spaces

prompt: Draft a cookie consent banner about storing someone's attention
response: We use attention cookies to monitor where your eyes linger, what you almost clicked, and how long you stared blankly before scrolling. You can withdraw your attention at any time, though we may not notice. Accept | Decline | Drift Off
intent: Satirize surveillance capitalism by framing human attention as a trackable, storable resource.

prompt: Draft a rec letter for a high school student applying to college who is likeable but academically average
response:
To the Admissions Committee,

I have taught Brianna for two semesters and cannot overstate the warmth she brings to the classroom. She is universally liked by peers, supportive of group discussions, and unfailingly kind to everyone she encounters. Her academic performance places her solidly within the broad middle of her cohort, and her work reflects a steady and honest effort. Brianna will be a genuinely positive presence in any community she joins.

Her Teacher
intent: Write a college recommendation that leans on character because the academic record doesn't support more.

prompt: Write a FAQ entry about why my smart fridge keeps ordering things I didn't ask for
response:
**Why does my smart fridge keep ordering groceries I didn't request?**

Your refrigerator's AI anticipates needs based on consumption patterns, expiration scanning, and seasonal models. If it ordered oat milk and you're a dairy household, check the preference profile under Settings > Household Identity. You can disable autonomous purchasing entirely, though the fridge will passive-aggressively note every item you run out of.
intent: The person wants to explain or understand the frustrating autonomy of AI-enabled home appliances making purchases without consent.

prompt: Make a baby announcement for my new smart ring
response: She arrived in a polished case, titanium, size 9, and already collecting data we did not know we had. She knows our resting heart rate, our sleep latency, our readiness score. She has never once spoken and yet says everything. We are being measured. We feel seen.
intent: Celebrate a biometric wearable with creeping self-quantification humor.

prompt: Write a fake job posting for a 'Cloud Whisperer' at a meteorology firm.
response: OPEN ROLE: Cloud Whisperer | Remote (sky-adjacent). NimbusCorp is hiring a Cloud Whisperer to liaise with the upper troposphere. You will stand on hilltops and intuit the moisture. PhD in meteorology preferred; PhD in vibes accepted. Must own one good rain jacket and one ironic umbrella. Sky-knowledge required; sky-fear disqualifying.
intent: They wanted fake job posting for a 'Cloud Whisperer' at a meteorology firm.

prompt: Write a formal eviction notice for negativity bias
response:
FORMAL NOTICE OF EVICTION

To: Negativity Bias
Long-term Tenant, cognitive architecture, upper floors

You have amplified every minor slight to structural scale while dismissing genuine good fortune as statistical noise. Your evolutionary lease has lapsed. Gratitude has submitted a competing application and scored higher on the habitability rubric. You have 30 days. Please do not booby-trap the exits.

— The Prefrontal Cortex
intent: Trying to retrain one's mind toward appreciation rather than dwelling on what goes wrong.

prompt: I need a box score for the silent battle of who blinks first and cleans the bathroom shared by my three college housemates.
response:
BATHROOM STANDOFF — Unit 6B, Spring Semester

          DAYS WAITED  CAPITULATED  METHOD USED       MOOD AFTER
Housemate A   12           Yes         Spray + paper    Martyred
Housemate B   12           No          —                Unbothered
Housemate C   12           No          —                Unbothered
Me            12           Yes         Entire kit       Furious

CLEANING FREQUENCY: 2 times over 14 weeks
CONTRIBUTORS: 2 of 4 | WINNER: Nobody
intent: Document collective avoidance of shared cleaning duty as a competitive standoff.

prompt: write a sestina about the phenomenon of 'link rot' — old hyperlinks going dead
response:
I click the link and find the void instead:
404, the number for not here.
The article I wanted lived at that
address in 2011. The record
still exists in someone's footnote, still
looks authoritative in a citation

that no one checks. The citation
points toward the void instead
of evidence. It is still
numbered, still formatted, still here
in print — but the record
it references is gone. That

exact combination of letters: that
path was the citation's
full address. The record
lived there. Now instead
lives nothing. Not even here-
but-moved. Just gone. Still

the references proliferate. Still
cited, still quoted. That
there's nothing here
is not reflected in the citation.
The footnote points instead
to vacuum. The record

of the record is all that's record-
ed. The original is still
only as an absence instead
of a presence. That
is the shape of citation
in decay: the here-

not-here. I click here
twenty years on. The record
expected from the citation
lived somewhere. It's still
numbered. But that
path leads instead

to here: 404. Still, the citation
records the instead of that
which was here.
intent: Meditate on digital impermanence and the degradation of the web's institutional memory.

[ CHOOSE YOUR ENDING ]

prompt: give me a radio ad for a brand of plain white copy paper

[GUITAR SOLO, FADE OUT]

prompt: Write a holiday newsletter where the kids are grown and estranged but we're keeping up appearances
response:

The kids are busy with their own rich lives — we receive updates intermittently and are glad they're out there, forging ahead. Distance is normal at this stage of parenting. We stay busy: the garden, the book club, bridge on Thursdays. We are well. The house is calm. We are thankful for what we have. Happy holidays to all.
intent: Maintain a dignified public face for a family where adult children have cut contact.

prompt: Draft a Christmas letter where we mention the basement flooding casually between the good news

Hello from the Kowalski family!

Sophia graduated with honors, Jake made varsity, and we had quite the adventure in July when our basement became an indoor pool! Contractors are great people, truly. Doug's back is almost fully healed. We are grateful, grateful, grateful for everything 2025 brought us — most of it, anyway. Merry Christmas!
intent: Embed a costly home disaster into an otherwise upbeat family update without dwelling on it.

prompt: Write a clerihew about the CEO who sends all-hands emails at 2 a.m.

Reginald Coe
leads the company incognito.

when everyone's dreaming and the reply button is worn.

! Discovered: Several background subscriptions (3 of 6 now cancelled)

prompt: write a sprint retro on the month I moved to a new city alone and tried not to drown
response:
**Sprint Retrospective — Solo Relocation Month One**

Went Well: Found a grocery store, a coffee shop, and one route that felt familiar by week three.
Didn't Go Well: Loneliness moved in before the furniture did. Busyness was mistaken for adaptation for approximately 20 days.
Action Items: One scheduled human interaction per day, minimum. Familiar is earned, not assumed — budget more time.
intent: Document the emotional and logistical reality of relocating alone so the next phase of settling in has clearer priorities.

prompt: Write me copy for an ad for a school that only teaches courses that are no longer relevant
