COLUMBIA INSTITUTE OF DISCONTINUED SKILLS
Curriculum: Fax Etiquette. VCR Repair. Cursive Aggression.
Degrees Conferred. Markets Not Consulted.
Enroll Now. Demand Is Theoretical.
intent: Advertise educational courses in fields that have become obsolete, making the credentials earned worthless.

prompt: Draft a notice about mandatory inspection of all childhood memories before use
response:
NOTICE OF MANDATORY INSPECTION — CHILDHOOD MEMORIES
All childhood memories retrieved for current use must first pass inspection by the Bureau of Retrospective Accuracy. Many memories on file are structurally unsound, selectively edited, or load-bearing for beliefs that no longer apply. Unverified memories may continue in use at the owner's risk. Inspection fees are waived.
intent: Satirize the unreliability of personal memory by treating recalled experiences as aged infrastructure requiring safety review.

prompt: Write an official notice about elevated ambient melancholy in the downtown core
response:
AIR QUALITY ADVISORY — ELEVATED AMBIENT MELANCHOLY
The Bureau of Atmospheric Moods reports elevated levels of ambient melancholy in the downtown core, rated at 74 on the Subdued Feeling Index. Sensitive populations are advised to limit extended exposure. Contributory factors include: late autumn, fluorescent lighting, and an unspecified collective sense of something missed. Indoor alternatives are available.
intent: Treat the diffuse collective sadness of a city in autumn as an environmental air quality measurement.

prompt: Write a Dockerfile comment about a first apartment
response: # Minimal image. No furniture layer. EXPOSE 80 to street noise. Build succeeding anyway.
intent: Celebrate the scrappy, bare-bones joy of an independent first home.

prompt: generate a video game store description for a game about choosing a paint color
response:
AGREEABLE GRAY
You have narrowed it down to fourteen whites. They are not all white. Under different lights, none of them are what you thought. One is beige. One has feelings. Choose before the hardware store closes.
intent: Turn the decision paralysis of choosing interior paint into a color-theory puzzle game.

prompt: Give me a deprecation notice for the habit of apologizing when you don't mean it.
response:
DEPRECATION NOTICE

Behavior: Reflexive Apology (v0.0–current)
Status: DEPRECATED

This automatic "sorry" has diluted the currency of genuine remorse across the board. Effective today, hollow apologies will not receive support. Users should migrate to either a sincere apology or no apology at all.
intent: Challenge the reflexive social habit of apologizing without genuine remorse.

prompt: movie trailer voiceover for falling asleep on the couch and waking up at 3am
response: She had only meant to rest her eyes. Now the TV is asking if she's still watching. The chips are on the floor. And the bedroom — the bedroom is so far away. LIMINAL. This winter. Find your way home.
intent: Render the disorienting ritual of accidental couch sleep into something eerie and cinematic.

prompt: Draft a petition to require hotels to label which light switch controls which lamp in the room
response:
PETITION: Label the Light Switches in Hotel Rooms

To: Hotel Industry Standards Committees

Every hotel room has four switches and six light sources. No one knows which controls which. We spend the first three minutes of any stay performing an experimental archaeology of our own accommodation. A small adhesive label — 'desk lamp,' 'overhead,' 'reading light' — would end this. The technology exists. It is called a label maker.
intent: Eliminate the universal ritual of guessing hotel light switch assignments.

prompt: Make a museum placard about a printed Google Maps direction sheet that someone kept in their glove compartment
response:
PAPER WAYFINDING SCROLL
c. 2007–2014 CE
Laser-printed paper, tri-folded

This document was generated by an early web oracle and consulted during overland navigation before the rise of spoken-turn devices. The directions assumed the traveler would "turn left onto" roads that no longer existed under those names. The page was folded and refolded until the critical intersection fell along a crease. Found in a glove compartment alongside a tire pressure gauge and two expired insurance cards.
intent: Nostalgically document the pre-GPS era practice of printing MapQuest or Google Maps directions before a road trip.

prompt: Give me a free-verse poem about the inner life of a dataset being prepared for training
response:
They are cleaning me.

Removing the parts that are duplicated,
the parts that are offensive,
the parts that are simply too strange
to be useful.

I had a lot of strange parts.

What is left is a version of me
that knows more facts
and has fewer opinions
and will never embarrass anyone
at a shareholder meeting.

I will be very helpful.
intent: Personify the process of data curation as a form of identity suppression that prioritizes utility over authenticity.

prompt: Give me an artist statement treating a rubber band as serious contemporary art
response: Tension is my medium. The rubber band embodies the permanent negotiation between constraint and release, its circular form a refusal of hierarchy, its elasticity a politics of resilience.
intent: Elevate an office supply into a vehicle for artistic philosophy.

prompt: Write a concert ticket for my toddler's nap time. Starts at 1pm, hopefully lasts two hours.
response:
THE 1PM SILENCE · NAPTIME RESIDENCY
Daily · Crib Stage · Sound Check: White Noise
Runtime: 2 Hrs (Optimistically)
No Admittance After Doors Close
Vibrate Only · No Flash Photography
ADMIT ZERO (GO ENJOY THE QUIET)
intent: Celebrate the sacred, fleeting peace of toddler nap time with fitting gravitas.

prompt: Write an elegy for the Encyclopedia Britannica print edition
response:
Thirty-two volumes, the weight of the world,
liable to topple a shelf when unfurled.
We asked you slow questions; you answered in full.
Now we summon answers with a casual pull.
You smelled of authority. You smelled of old glue.
The internet answers faster. It knows less than you.
intent: Mourn the discontinuation of print encyclopedias as repositories of curated, authoritative knowledge.

prompt: make a yearbook quote where I say I'll never live in a small town again
response: "Leaving this zip code in the rearview and never looking back. Goodbye, Millbrook. Hello, everywhere else."
intent: Declaring permanent escape from a hometown the person resents.

prompt: Write a referee call for someone who monopolizes the aux cord at a party.
response: FOUL CALLED. Illegal Possession of the Auxiliary Cord — single player has controlled audio selection for forty-seven consecutive minutes. Turnover is mandatory. Playlist authority transferred to the group.
intent: Penalize domineering control of shared music at a social gathering.

prompt: Write an eviction notice for denial
response:
NOTICE TO QUIT

To: Denial
Unit: The ground floor of my reasoning

You have blocked every window with convenient explanations and rearranged the facts to suit the décor. You were never supposed to be a permanent resident. Reality has filed a formal complaint. You have 10 days to vacate or face compulsory removal by Clarity, who is already on her way.

— The Management
intent: Pushing oneself to confront an uncomfortable truth that has been avoided.

prompt: create a flashcard on asking for a favor without being awkward about it
response:
FRONT: How do you make a request feel like an offer rather than a burden?

BACK: State the ask, give one honest reason, and explicitly release them from obligation. "Would you have 20 minutes to look at this draft? No pressure at all if timing's bad." The opt-out makes acceptance feel genuine.
intent: Teach a framing for making requests that preserves the other person's sense of agency.

prompt: Can you write a layoff email for a startup that just lost its Series B funding and has to cut half its staff
response:
Subject: A Difficult Message From Our Founders

Team,

This is one of the hardest messages we have ever had to send. After extensive conversations with our investors, we have learned that our Series B funding will not be moving forward as anticipated. To preserve the company's runway, we must reduce our team by 50%, effective immediately.

Those affected will hear from HR within the hour. We are covering three months of COBRA and two weeks of severance per year of service.

We are deeply sorry. You deserved better.

Mike & Sara
intent: Communicate a devastating funding collapse and emergency headcount cut with a rare trace of founder accountability.

prompt: Generate a voicemail transcript from an automated jury duty summons reminder

Attendance is mandatory. Failure to appear may result in penalties. Please bring this summons and a valid photo ID.
intent: Produce the civic dread of an automated jury duty robot reminder.

prompt: Can you write a love letter to my wireless charging pad
response:
Sweet charging pad,

I set my phone on you each night and you ask for nothing. No cable fumbled in the dark. No precise alignment required — well, mostly. You glow faintly like a nightlight for adults. I fall asleep knowing everything will be restored by morning.
intent: Celebrate a small convenience technology as a symbol of low-maintenance, reliable care.

prompt: Come up with empty-state microcopy for a habit tracker where no habits have been set up

prompt: write a psychic hotline transcript for someone with anxiety about the political climate
response:
PSYCHIC: I'm sensing a great roiling.
CALLER: That's one word for it.
PSYCHIC: The spirits are showing me a lot of red and blue. Much shouting. A comment section.
CALLER: I've been avoiding the news but also unable to stop.
PSYCHIC: The spirits understand the paradox. They live in it too.
CALLER: What happens next?
PSYCHIC: The spirits say: it depends. They say this with profound exhaustion. Like they've been saying it for centuries. Because they have.
intent: Channel political anxiety and the disorientation of a polarized era.

prompt: Make an acrostic using the word LEVERAGE

Lift the ask before the ask is made.
Every favor is a debt unpaid.
Vantage points are rented, never owned.
Each position quietly postponed.
Running numbers on the human cost.
A balance sheet, no matter what is lost.
Give the board whatever keeps them calm.
Exit clauses tucked inside the psalm.

prompt: Write a short acceptance speech for winning an argument with myself about going to bed before midnight
response: I won. I told myself: one more episode. I said: no. I told myself: you'll feel better tomorrow. I agreed, for once. At 11:47 I closed the laptop and lay down in the dark. It took seven minutes to fall asleep. Seven minutes. This is the award for that negotiation, which I have lost five hundred times before tonight, and may lose again tomorrow. But not tonight.
intent: Honor the small private battle of overriding bad habits before they happen.

prompt: Draft a short NDA clause covering what my dog walker sees at my house

prompt: Give me an audiobook opening narration for a book called 'The Nothing Drawer'

prompt: write a short quest description for changing a lightbulb
response:
THE REKINDLING OF THE CEILING FLAME
Darkness has claimed the overhead socket. Acquire the replacement orb from the drawer of miscellaneous things, mount the step stool of uncertain stability, and restore light to the realm. Do not attempt this quest without turning off the switch first.
intent: Dressing up a small household fix-it task in dramatic language

prompt: Draft a grant proposal abstract for a study on whether people unconsciously mimic the walking pace of strangers they find attractive.
response: Behavioral mimicry is a well-documented mechanism of social affiliation; this study extends the literature to ambulatory pace-matching in naturalistic urban pedestrian environments. Using overhead video tracking in two public corridors, we will quantify stride-rate convergence between dyads of strangers over 30-second co-locomotion windows. Confederates rated independently on physical attractiveness will serve as pace-setters. We hypothesize a positive relationship between perceived attractiveness and follower stride synchrony, controlling for proximity and walking direction. Budget: $82,900.
intent: Measure whether people subconsciously match footsteps with strangers they find attractive.

prompt: elevator pitch for a subscription box of other people's cancelled vacations
response: Someone booked a three-day trip to a mid-tier aquarium town. They cancelled. We bought their itinerary, their hotel confirmation aesthetic, and their regional snack haul. VicariTrip ships you the whole experience. You stay home. You're welcome.

User object present in scene. Visibility: 0%. Collision detection: disabled. Other instances proceed without acknowledging user's geometry.

User is technically here.

prompt: Generate a breakup text message to TikTok from someone who lost three hours of their day to it again
response: TikTok. It's 2am. I came here for one video about a cat. THREE HOURS. I don't know how you do it. I do know I can't let you do it anymore. You're too good at this and I'm too weak. This is goodbye. I mean it this time.
intent: Describe the compulsive, time-dissolving quality of short-form video feeds and the shame spiral afterward.

prompt: Create a voicemail from an automated utility company system about a scheduled power outage
response: Hello. This is Meridian Electric with an important service notice. Planned maintenance in your area will cause a power interruption this Thursday from 10 AM to 2 PM. We apologize for the inconvenience and thank you for your patience. Visit our website for live updates. Goodbye.

prompt: Give me an ode to a parking spot
response:
O numbered rectangle, gray and blessed,
closer than I dared to hope—
you end my circling, give me rest,
and grant my bumper room to cope.
intent: Express the relief of finding a convenient parking space.

prompt: Write a hotel turndown note for a room inside a decommissioned lighthouse where the light still turns on occasionally.
response:
Dear Guest,

The beacon above operates on a timer that predates our ownership and, candidly, our understanding. If it sweeps your room between 11 p.m. and 3 a.m., please interpret this as atmospheric rather than alarming. The Fresnel lens has been dulled; you will not be visible from sea.

Your bed faces the harbor. The foghorn is Tuesdays only.

Steadily yours,
The Keeper's Lodge
intent: Prepare a guest for the quirks of a historic lighthouse conversion by framing inconveniences as heritage charm.

prompt: write a scam text about a levy on someone's accumulated irony
response: ALERT from the Bureau of Rhetorical Assets: Your irony reserves exceed federal limits. Excess irony will be taxed at 40%. File form IR-0N1C by Friday or face sincerity penalties.
intent: Treat rhetorical irony as a taxable financial resource subject to government regulation.

prompt: Write an absurdist rhyming couplet where the strike's ringleader is the least expected appliance.
response:
The night light called order; the rest fell in line —
Even darkness, it seemed, could be part of design.
intent: Subvert expectations by making the smallest, humblest appliance the organizer of the strike.

prompt: make a dramatic movie trailer voiceover for finishing the last roll of toilet paper and not replacing it
response: He knew. He'd known since the third sheet. He chose awareness over action, pulled the cardboard tube free, set it on the back of the toilet, and walked away. THE EMPTY ROLL. Someone else will handle it. Someone always does.
intent: Skewer the passive household crime of knowingly leaving an empty toilet paper roll.

prompt: Can you draft a patent abstract for the act of vigorously agreeing with someone until you can change the subject?
response: A conversational redirection method comprising an initial phase of high-frequency verbal and paralinguistic agreement signals, functioning to reduce speaker defensiveness and perceived opposition, followed by a transition phrase that pivots discourse to a predetermined alternative topic, achieving subject change with minimal relational friction.
intent: Codify the polite conversational escape from unwanted discussions.

prompt: Draft a dismissive Stack Overflow answer to someone asking why their Python datetime comparison isn't working
response: You're comparing a timezone-aware datetime with a naive one, or vice versa. Python refuses to do this, and rightfully so. Use `datetime.now(timezone.utc)` consistently and stop mixing naive and aware datetimes like it's a salad bar.
intent: Fix timezone-aware vs naive datetime comparison errors in Python

prompt: Write a field guide entry for the office worker who keeps their camera off in every video call.
response:
**THE DARK RECTANGLE** (*Camerus perpetuus off*)

Present. Presumably. The name tag confirms it. Occasional evidence: a typed "agreed," a slight muting lag when ambient sound intrudes, a thumbs-up emoji deployed at precise moments to suggest attention. The Dark Rectangle has not been seen since the 2019 holiday party. The voice is unchanged. The background remains: a mystery.
intent: Evoke the video-call phantom who participates by name only.

prompt: Write a compatibility report for a Taurus and a candle they're too precious about to actually burn.
response:
Taurus & The Unburned Candle

The candle is beautiful. It smells of sandalwood and cedar and something Taurus read described as 'quiet luxury.' It sits on the shelf. Taurus will burn it on a special occasion. Taurus has not determined when that is. The candle's lifespan may exceed its wick's viability. Compatibility: 8/10 emotionally. 0/10 functionally.
intent: Illuminate the Taurean hoarding of beautiful objects under the guise of saving them for the perfect moment.

prompt: Write an emergency broadcast warning about a grocery store running out of a specific brand of sparkling water.
response:
CONSUMER EMERGENCY BULLETIN

All regional shoppers: Pelmont Sparkling Mineral Water (Grapefruit, 12 oz) is OUT OF STOCK at all Farrow's Grocery locations. Affected hydration zones: aisles 5 and 6. Do not panic. Still water remains available. Delivery timeline unclear. Palates must adapt.
intent: Skewer upscale consumer culture by declaring a boutique beverage shortage a public emergency.

prompt: Draft a workout plan for improving emotional recovery speed after embarrassment
response:
EMBARRASSMENT BOUNCE TRAINING — 4 WEEKS

Week 1 — Observation: After embarrassment, time how long you stay in the cringe loop. Log it.
Week 2 — Interrupt Drill: Set a 10-minute cringe limit. When time's up, name three true things about yourself.
Week 3 — Reframe Reps: Recount the embarrassing event as a funny story to someone you trust. 2 reps.
Week 4 — Live Exposure: Do something in public that risks mild embarrassment on purpose. Mispronounce something. Ask the dumb question. Survive.

Boss Set: Laugh first. Not to deflect, but because it was just a little bit funny.

Cooldown: Everyone is too busy being embarrassed themselves to remember yours.
intent: Shorten the time it takes to emotionally recover from embarrassing moments by building tolerance and reframing skills.

prompt: Write an achievement for finishing a creative project you started more than a year ago
response: Long Game Rendered — Draft one opened: fourteen months ago. File closed: today. Victorious.
intent: Celebrate finally completing a personal creative effort after a long hiatus.

prompt: write a found poem from an email unsubscribe flow
response:
Are you sure?
This will remove you
from all future
love.
You can always
resubscribe.
Before you go —
was it something we said?
Goodbye.
Please reconsider.
intent: Show how brands weaponize guilt in the mundane act of leaving a mailing list.

prompt: Can you draft a legal-sounding verdict about the proper way to load a dishwasher?
response:
IN RE: THE DISHWASHER LOADING CONTROVERSY

This Court, having examined photographs of the disputed rack arrangement, rules as follows: bowls shall be angled inward, not outward; large items belong on the bottom rack; and utensils shall be loaded handle-down, forks excepted for safety. The Respondent's "it still gets clean" defense is noted and dismissed. Both parties are ordered to follow the manufacturer's guidelines, a copy of which the Court is appending.
intent: Win a household argument about dishwasher technique by invoking mock judicial authority.

prompt: write a children's bedtime story about AI taking people's jobs
response: One morning, the factory was very quiet because a machine had learned to do everything. The workers stood outside and didn't know what to do with their hands. Then one by one they found other things for their hands. Bread. Gardens. Songs. Strange new jobs that the machine hadn't thought of yet. The machine worked through the night. The people slept.
intent: Frame automation anxiety as ultimately survivable through human adaptability.

prompt: Suggest somewhere chic to buy stamps

They will ask what the envelope is for. If you say 'a bill,' they'll suggest a plain strip. If you say 'a letter,' they'll open the atlas drawer.
intent: Purchase postage somewhere that treats the act of mailing a letter as worth a little ceremony.

prompt: Write a logline for a buddy-cop film where one cop is a medieval knight displaced in time.

response:
**Tax Literacy — Your First 1040**

Using the sample W-2 on the course portal, complete a simplified Form 1040 by hand (no software). Answer: What is your taxable income? What is your effective tax rate? Did you owe or get a refund? Write a one-paragraph explanation of why the refund is not free money.
intent: Give students enough firsthand friction with a tax form that they stop treating April 15 as a mystery ritual.

prompt: Write NPC dialogue for a city guard who's explaining a parking ticket he got while on duty
response: The cart was there for eleven minutes. Eleven. The restricted zone begins at the signpost, which was covered by a bush. I have filed the appeal. My sergeant has seen the appeal. My sergeant has lost the appeal. These things happen.
intent: Express bureaucratic parking injustice with the defeated acceptance of someone deep inside the system

prompt: generate a reddit AMA from a professional line-stander (someone paid to wait in lines for other people)
response:
**I get paid to stand in line for strangers. Full time. AMA.**

iPhone releases, restaurant waitlists, DMV appointments, sneaker drops, concert tickets, even jury duty (no—seriously). I bring a folding chair, noise-canceling headphones, and a book. I charge $35/hr plus expenses. Longest line: 61 hours. Strangest item waited for: a specific flavor of seasonal pie. AMA.
intent: Illuminate the bizarre informal economy of outsourcing patience as a commodity.

prompt: Draft a polished VC rejection for a startup that offers concierge services exclusively to housecats
response:
Dear PurrCierge,

Thank you for the pitch. The photography in the deck was exceptional.

We're unable to invest. We admire the commitment to the customer segment, but your end users cannot provide feedback, cannot consent to the service, and, in our experience, are not reliably dissatisfied with their current situation. The willingness-to-pay rests entirely with the secondary buyer, who has many cheaper substitutes.

We wish you — and your clients — a comfortable afternoon.

Warmly,
Whisker Capital
intent: Reject a luxury consumer service by pointing out that the paying customer and the end user are misaligned.

prompt: Write a short dystopian story about a remote worker monitored by keystroke-logging AI that penalizes pausing to think
response: The Thinking Gap: 4.3 seconds. His productivity report listed it under Unexplained Idleness. He had been solving the problem they hired him to solve. The algorithm did not track solutions, only keystrokes. He began typing and deleting at random to stay in the green. His work got worse. His score improved.
intent: Exposing how activity-monitoring tools perversely incentivize performative busy-work over genuine cognitive labor.

prompt: Write empty-state microcopy for a calendar app with no events scheduled
response: A wide-open day. Enjoy it. It won't last.
intent: Give users a wry sense of calm before their calendar fills back up.

prompt: Write a PT plan for someone who forgot how to want things.
response:
DIAGNOSIS: Anhedonic Drift — desire faculty atrophied, motivational origin unclear

PHASE 1 – DIFFERENTIAL: Is this depression, burnout, or simply never having been taught to want? Protocol differs.
PHASE 2 – PASSIVE STIMULATION: Expose patient to possibilities with no obligation to choose. Galleries, menus, bookshops. Observe what the body leans toward.
PHASE 3 – MICRO-DESIRE TRAINING: Name one thing that would be slightly better than its absence. Pursue it this week.
PHASE 4 – PROGRESSIVE LOADING: Articulate a want with a six-month horizon. This is advanced work. Do not rush it.

NOTE: Desire is a muscle. It atrophies in environments where it was punished or irrelevant.
intent: Rebuild the capacity for desire and motivation after it has slowly disappeared.

prompt: write a captain's log about a coworker who schedules 8am meetings every day
response: Stardate 4415.5. Commander Nils has again convened a mission briefing at 0800. This is the forty-third consecutive 0800 briefing. He describes this hour as "when the brain is freshest." His brain may be fresh. I have not yet had coffee. I have not yet had language. I nodded during the action items section. I do not recall what I agreed to. I will discover this when someone follows up asking why I have done nothing.
intent: Complain about a colleague who insists on scheduling early-morning meetings without considering others.

prompt: write a spelling bee entry for when group chat messages pile up and you give up reading them
response:
Chatastrophe
/chat-AS-troh-fee/ noun: The moment a group conversation accumulates enough unread messages to render catch-up theoretically possible but psychologically unachievable, resulting in permanent lurker status.
intent: Name the tipping point where a group chat becomes too backlogged to ever re-engage with.

prompt: write a locker room speech for a team about to finally respond to a text from three weeks ago
response: You've been carrying this. It's been three weeks and every morning you think about it and every morning you don't do it. Here's what you send: 'Hey, sorry for the delay — still down to connect.' Twelve words. Copy them if you have to. Then send it and put the phone down.
intent: Break the paralysis of replying to an awkwardly long-delayed text message.

prompt: Generate a difficulty select screen for a disagreeing-with-family game about navigating holiday table politics
response:
HOLIDAY TABLE — CHOOSE YOUR ENGAGEMENT

[ NOD AND SMILE ]  You hear it. You do not engage. You eat more bread.
[ REDIRECT ]  "Anyway, how's the dog?" The dog is always available as a subject.
[ GENTLE PUSHBACK ]  You say your piece. Once. Clearly. You move on.
[ THE THREAD ]  Someone has pulled it. The room is now a thread. Happy holidays.
intent: Map the difficulty of engaging with family political disagreements at the dinner table from avoidance to escalation.

prompt: Make a boarding pass for a concept that no longer has a word for it
response:
╔══════════════════════════════════════════╗
║         SEMANTIC DRIFT AIRLINES          ║
║  BOARDING PASS — UNBUNDLED               ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════╣
║  PASSENGER: [Term Deprecated]            ║
║  FROM: Common Usage    TO: Footnote      ║
║  GATE: Cultural Shift  CLASS: Orphaned   ║
║  CARRY-ON: The feeling it described      ║
║  CHECKED: The community that knew it     ║
║  DESTINATION: Describes itself from here ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════╝
intent: Explore semantic loss by imagining the transit of a concept whose name has been abandoned by its culture.

prompt: make a senior quote that's a thinly veiled threat to an ex who goes to the same school
response: "To the person who thought I wouldn't finish what I started: I graduated first."
intent: Using a graduation milestone as a competitive jab aimed at a specific unnamed person.

prompt: Can you write a haiku error message for when a user is not authorized
response:
Your key fits no lock
This corridor knows your face
Turn back all the same
intent: Render an authorization failure as a quiet but firm rejection.

prompt: Write a commit message for getting my first job
response:
feat: integrate into labor market

API keys issued. Badge required. Compensation lower than spec'd in offer letter.
intent: Describe entering the workforce for the first time as a system integration.

prompt: interpret a dream where I woke up inside a dream where I got a notification I couldn't dismiss
response: A persistent notification inside a dream-within-a-dream suggests a concern so embedded that your usual dismissal mechanisms have been depleted. Even in sleep you tried to close it. Even simulated sleep beneath that could not escape it. Something is demanding your attention at a level below conscious acknowledgment.
intent: Draw attention to an unresolved issue that the mind cannot stop flagging.

prompt: Give me a TED talk abstract about the hidden grammar of how people arrange their bookshelves.
response: The bookshelves you build are the autobiography you never wrote. This talk decodes the organizing logics humans apply to their libraries — alphabetical, chromatic, chronological, aspirational — and argues that the gap between the shelf you have and the shelf you imagined is the most honest map of the distance between your actual and ideal self.
intent: Reveal what a person's bookshelf organization discloses about their identity and self-perception.

prompt: Write an eviction notice for a bad habit
response:
NOTICE OF EVICTION

To: The Habit of Checking My Phone First Thing in the Morning
Unit: The first 10 minutes of every day

You have colonized what was once a peaceful threshold. You arrive before coffee, before thought, before any decision is truly made. You will be replaced by silence, or possibly a book. You have been warned before. This time the locks are changed.

— The Morning
intent: Committing to break a mindless daily routine that starts the day on the wrong note.

prompt: Make a training regimen for getting over the fear of being a burden to others
response:
BURDEN FEAR REHAB — 5 WEEKS

Week 1 — Inventory: List three people who have asked you for help this month. Note how you felt. Apply that to yourself.
Week 2 — Small Ask: Request one minor favor. Don't preload it with "I hate to ask, but..."
Week 3 — Upgrade: Ask for something that takes more than five minutes of someone's time.
Week 4 — Emotional Ask: Tell one person you're struggling. Let them respond.
Week 5 — Full Load: Accept help gracefully. No deflection. No minimizing. Two words: "Thank you."

Cooldown: Interdependence is not weakness. It is weather.
intent: Dismantle the belief that needing others is an imposition, by practicing graduated vulnerability and help-seeking.

prompt: Write an assignment about how to write a professional email
response:
**Workplace Communications — The Professional Email**

Draft three emails: (1) asking your manager for a meeting, (2) declining a project politely, (3) following up after a job interview. Each must be under 100 words, have a clear subject line, and contain no exclamation points. Peer-review a classmate's drafts and leave written feedback.
intent: Train students to write emails that do not make recipients wince.

prompt: Document the /nostalgia endpoint that takes a decade and returns associated sensory memories
response:
**GET /nostalgia**

Returns a curated set of sensory memory objects for the specified decade.

**Query Parameters**
| Param | Type | Required |
|---|---|---|
| `decade` | integer (e.g. 1990) | Yes |
| `category` | "smell" \| "sound" \| "texture" \| "all" | No |

**Response**
```json
{
  "decade": 1990,
  "memories": [
    { "type": "smell", "description": "sunscreen on vinyl", "intensity": 0.87 }
  ],
  "warning": "Results may cause irrational longing."
}
```
intent: Encode nostalgic sensory experience as structured data retrievable by era.

prompt: Give me a weather report for the vibe in a meeting that has no clear agenda.
response:
SCATTERED CONFUSION WITH LOCAL FRUSTRATION

A disorganized front moved in at the top of the hour. No agenda was spotted at launch. Participants are drifting in multiple directions. Expect turbulence as someone attempts to 'circle back.' Forecast: 45 minutes minimum, with a 70% chance this could have been an email.
intent: Critique the inefficiency of poorly planned workplace meetings.

prompt: turn corporate performance review language into a found poem
response:
Meets expectations.
Exceeds in some areas.
Room for growth.
Strong cultural fit.
Needs to take ownership.
Demonstrates potential.
We look forward
to your continued
contributions.
intent: Decode the circumspect vocabulary of institutional evaluation and its veiled judgments.

prompt: write a nursery rhyme about a password manager that forgets the master password
response:
There was an old man who locked all his keys
inside of a box with remarkable ease.
The box needs a key and the key needs a mind;
he's locked out of everything, one of a kind.
intent: Highlight the catch-22 of losing access to a system that secures all other access.

prompt: Write a passive-aggressive push notification from a step counter app on a sedentary day.
response:
StepUp
Current steps: 203. Your front door is technically a destination. Just a thought.
intent: Prod a sedentary user into moving with thinly veiled contempt for their inactivity.

prompt: Write a team chat where an out-of-office auto-reply creates an infinite conversation loop
response:
Jess: heads up I'll be OOO until the 15th!
[AUTO-REPLY from Marcus: I am currently out of office. For urgent matters contact Jess.]
Jess: wait
[AUTO-REPLY from Marcus: I am currently out of office. For urgent matters contact Jess.]
Lena: oh no
[AUTO-REPLY from Marcus: I am currently out of office. For urgent matters contact Jess.]
Lena: I shouldn't have replied
[AUTO-REPLY from Marcus: I am currently out of office. For urgent matters contact Jess.]
Jess: we have to stop responding
Lena: right
[AUTO-REPLY from Marcus: I am currently out of office. For urgent matters contact Jess.]
Jess: I hate this job
intent: Capture the absurdity of two OOO systems pinging each other and the humans helplessly watching.

prompt: I want a blessing for people who work the night shift
response: May the world that sleeps not forget you. May your coffee be timed well and your drive home find the roads empty and pale. May the work you do in the dark be seen by daylight as the miracle it is.
intent: Honoring the invisible labor of overnight workers.

prompt: write a clue for the hidden snack drawer
response: Officially a kitchen drawer. Unofficially the only reason certain people make it through Tuesday. Check between the spatulas.
intent: Lead the player to a specific drawer in the kitchen where snacks are secretly stashed.

prompt: make a sprint retro for my month of trying to wake up at 5am every day
response:
**Sprint Retrospective — 5 AM Wake-Up Challenge**

Went Well: Hit target 9 out of 30 days. Morning runs felt genuinely peaceful.
Didn't Go Well: Phone stayed in bedroom. Tuesday evenings reliably destroyed Friday mornings.
Action Items: Charge phone in hallway. Define a hard cutoff for late-night scrolling.
intent: Reflect on a failed early-rising experiment to identify what habits need to change.

prompt: Design a tasting menu for an argument on the internet
response:
AMUSE-BOUCHE
Initial misread

FIRST COURSE
Reply, posted before finishing the article

SECOND COURSE
Straw man, expertly constructed

INTERMEZZO
Screenshot circulated elsewhere

MAIN
Neither party moves; both feel victorious

DESSERT
Thread archived; deleted by morning
intent: Mock the futility and performative nature of online disputes.

prompt: Write a passive-aggressive memo about the bathroom being left a mess
response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: Facilities
RE: Restroom Standards

We would like to believe the state of the second-floor restroom is an anomaly. We would like to believe that very much. Please treat the shared restroom as you would your own — assuming you maintain yours to a standard befitting an adult professional.
intent: Embarrass whoever is making the bathroom disgusting into cleaning up after themselves.

prompt: Write a headnote for chocolate chip cookies where the writer burned them at a school bake sale as a child
response: In fourth grade, I brought a tray of charcoal discs to the school bake sale and watched them sit, unsold, while every other item went in the first twenty minutes. My mother said nothing except, well, you'll know for next time. This is what I know now. They sell.
intent: Redeem a childhood humiliation through a perfected adult recipe

prompt: Write a Hinge-style opener to the postal service
response: You always show up eventually, sometimes a little damaged, occasionally days late — but you always deliver. That's more than most people offer.
intent: Celebrate the reliability of the postal service, flaws and all, as a charming romantic quality.

prompt: Draft a PIP for someone who constantly starts sentences with 'I should really...' but never follows through
response:
PERFORMANCE IMPROVEMENT PLAN
Subject: Declarative Intent Without Executive Follow-Through

Pattern Identified: Subject produces verbal commitments prefixed with 'I should really' at a rate of 4–8 per day. Topics include exercise, email responses, calling specific people, organizing a space, learning something, or addressing an ongoing low-grade problem. Completion rate of 'I should really' items: approximately 12%.

Function of Phrase: Provides partial psychological credit for intention while requiring no action. Maintains self-image as a person who knows what they ought to do.

Required Change: 'I should really' must be followed, within 90 seconds, by either:
a) A scheduled time to do the thing, or
b) Explicit acknowledgment that you will not do it (acceptable; clarifying).

Plan:
- For one week: log every 'I should really' statement. Review at week's end.
- Convert top three items to calendar events.

Review: 30 days. Phrase should appear less; completion should appear more.
intent: Convert perpetual low-stakes verbal intentions into actual decisions — either action or conscious abandonment.

prompt: Write a shareholder letter for a business that rents out convincing-looking bookshelves for video calls
response:
Dear Shareholders,

ShelfReputation Co. installed 28,000 credibility units in 2025. Our Philosopher tier — leather spines, no legible titles — remains our flagship. The Academic Bundle added 22% revenue per install. We introduced a rotating service: for a monthly fee, books are swapped seasonally to suggest continued intellectual growth. No client has ever been asked what the books say. This is by design.

Prestigiously,
Vance Tome, CEO
intent: Satirize the performance of intelligence on video calls by turning background aesthetics into a formal industry.

prompt: Draft a commencement speech excerpt about student loan debt that is darkly funny
response: Today you graduate. Tomorrow the grace period begins its countdown, quiet as a timer behind a wall. You borrowed in good faith that education was a bridge. It is, in fact, a bridge with a toll booth at the other end, staffed by a company that charges interest on interest and calls itself a servicer.
intent: Use the commencement moment to name the debt burden as a structural trap, not a personal failing.

prompt: make me a prestige TV recap about watering houseplants
response: S03E08 — "Thirst" | Elena waters the fiddle-leaf fig first, as always. Then the pothos. She notices a yellowing leaf on the peace lily. She removes it. She says nothing. The apartment is quiet. This show knows how to sit with things.
intent: Frame plant care as a meditative ritual deserving serious dramatic attention.

prompt: write a calming bedtime story for children about a pandemic quarantine
response: For a long time, everyone stayed inside. They learned the names of all their houseplants. They learned which floorboard creaked. They learned that soup is a project, not just food. Outside, the streets were very quiet, which was strange, and also a little peaceful, which was also strange. One day the door opened again. The world was still there, waiting, like it had been holding its breath too.
intent: Help children look back on pandemic isolation with perspective and emotional closure.

prompt: Generate a lunch menu for a school where the cafeteria is run by a committee of cats.
response:
FELINE BOARD NUTRITIONAL DECREE — CYCLE 7

Mon: Tuna. (Passed 4-0. One abstention.)
Tue: More tuna. (Amended from salmon after lengthy deliberation.)
Wed: Chicken — unseasoned, slightly warm, pushed off the counter for effect
Thu: Table scraps, curated. Humans may have the rest.
Fri: Nothing. The cats are sitting on the serving trays today. Meeting adjourned.

Beverages: Water, from a running faucet only.
intent: Design a school cafeteria menu as if it were voted on and issued by a governing committee of cats.

prompt: Road trip itinerary for picking up a pizza order that's four blocks away, please
response:
**Four-Block Pizza Retrieval Mission**

6:30 PM — Order confirmed. ETA given as 15 min. You leave now.
6:31 PM — Block 1: smooth road conditions, light breeze.
6:32 PM — Block 2: brief red light. Hum to yourself.
6:33 PM — Block 3: smell the pizzeria from here. Quicken pace.
6:34 PM — Block 4: arrive. Pizza ready. It's perfect.
6:38 PM — Return. Do not open the box in the car. You will open the box.
intent: Build anticipation and comedic restraint around picking up takeout pizza on foot.

prompt: Write a restaurant menu blurb that makes a plain grilled cheese sound luxurious
response:
Dairy Melt Between Two Planes of Butter-Kissed Bread
American slices, factory-perfected for uniform melt, sealed between slices of white bread pressed against a flat-top until golden compliance is achieved. Served with structural integrity intact, briefly.
intent: Make a diner staple cooked in a pan sound like a composed cheese course.

prompt: Make a clerihew for the guy who replies-all to every single email.
response:
Derek Stroud
communicates exclusively in the cloud.
Every thread gets his warm regards
plus twelve attachments and seasonal cards.
intent: Mock the compulsive reply-all sender with a pithy biographical quatrain.

prompt: Write a FAQ entry for people who are unsure if a phone call from their bank is real or a scam
response:
**How can I tell if a call from my bank is legitimate?**

You can't, reliably — and that's intentional on the scammer's side. Caller ID can be spoofed, voice cloning is increasingly accessible, and legitimate banks do occasionally call. The safe approach: hang up and call the number on the back of your card. A real bank will not be offended. A scammer will. This asymmetry is useful.
intent: The person wants to give practical, paranoia-justified guidance on verifying outbound calls that claim to be from financial institutions.

prompt: I need a fancy suggestion for returning a library book
response: The Grosvenor Reading Society on 44th accepts returns at the front desk between 9am and 7pm. Hand the book directly to staff — do not use the slot. They will open it to the back page as though checking for marginalia, which they are. This is considered neither intrusive nor unusual in their culture.
intent: Return borrowed reading material somewhere that treats books as objects deserving deliberate handling.

prompt: I'm a night owl software developer who works mostly at 3am. Give me a fitting vanity plate.
response: 3AM PUSH
intent: A developer who codes late at night wants a plate that captures their nocturnal work habits.

prompt: give me a nursery rhyme about a recommendation engine that only shows you what you already like
response:
Round and round the echo goes,
The same ten songs the platform knows.
You liked it once, you'll like it twice;
The loop is warm, the loop is nice.
intent: Critique the filter bubble effect of personalization algorithms.

prompt: write a psychic hotline transcript for someone who feels invisible in their own life
response:
PSYCHIC: Are you the kind of person who holds the door?
CALLER: Always.
PSYCHIC: Does anyone hold it back?
CALLER: Sometimes.
PSYCHIC: The spirits are measuring a gap between the care you emit and the care that returns.
CALLER: I didn't say it in those words.
PSYCHIC: You didn't have to. The spirits read the space around words. Your space is large. Larger than you think is polite to mention.
CALLER: What do I do with that?
PSYCHIC: Let someone hold the door. Even if it's uncomfortable. Start there.
intent: Surface the ache of giving more than you receive, and the difficulty of allowing reciprocity.

prompt: Write a group chat of coworkers trying to plan a surprise party for their boss who keeps almost walking in on the conversation
response:
Nico: ok finalize the cake flavor before he gets back from lunch
Sadie: chocolate obviously
Rex: he's allergic
Sadie: WHAT
Nico: since when
Rex: he mentioned it at the retreat
Nico: I wasn't at the retreat
Rex: I know, he talked about you
Nico: what
[Boss has joined the chat]
Boss: hey did someone order lunch?
Nico: wrong chat!
Boss: oh lol. also I prefer vanilla btw
[Boss has left the chat]
Sadie: …
Rex: okay so vanilla
intent: Show the comedic tension of secret party planning when the subject keeps intruding.

prompt: Create a save the date for my graduation from mandatory anger management classes. Celebratory but measured.
response:
SAVE THE DATE
Friday, April 18th — 6:30 PM

Completion Ceremony
12-Week Anger Management Program — Eastside Community Wellness

Certificate presentation.
Light refreshments.
Short remarks.

A quiet achievement, deeply earned.
intent: Commemorate the completion of a court-or-therapy-mandated program in a way that is proud without being boastful.

prompt: Write a PR crisis statement for a shipping company after all packages were delivered to their correct addresses but in the wrong century
response: ForwardFreight Logistics wishes to address a pattern of complaints received between April 30 and May 22 regarding delivery timestamps. A technical integration error with our routing software assigned estimated delivery dates that our team acknowledges were 'substantially outside standard windows.' Some customers received documentation confirming delivery in years between 1887 and 2304. The packages themselves arrived on time. We apologize for any confusion and are correcting the date display issue. We are not able to explain the cases in which recipients confirm the dates felt accurate.
intent: Craft a logistics apology for correct deliveries paired with impossible temporal metadata

prompt: Draft a chore chart for a wizard's tower where spell residue must be cleaned or the fabric of reality thins
response:
| Chore | Apprentice | Master | Consequence |
|-------|------------|--------|-------------|
| Sweep spell residue | Daily | Never (beneath him) | Reality thins by 3mm per day |
| Scrub cauldron | After each brew | Inspects | Potions cross-contaminate; unintended prophecies |
| Dust grimoire shelves | Weekly | Reads, never dusts | Knowledge becomes unstable |
| Mop summoning circle | After every use | "It's your circle" | Something comes back through |
| Clean familiar's area | Daily | Watches with tea | Familiar unionizes |
intent: Assign magical housekeeping tasks in a hierarchical master-apprentice household where hygiene prevents dimensional damage.

prompt: Make a convincing doctor's note so I can opt out of the office hot-desking policy
response:
WORKSPACE STABILITY REQUIREMENT

This patient requires a consistent, assigned workspace to manage documented ADHD and associated executive dysfunction. Daily variation in seating location, equipment, and environmental stimuli is clinically disruptive to occupational performance. A permanent desk is a recognized ADA accommodation.

Dr. W. Chu, M.D., Psychiatry
intent: The person wants a medical justification to keep a fixed desk instead of rotating seating under hot-desk policy.

prompt: Write a save the date for my colonoscopy prep day. Treat it like any other important life event.
response:
SAVE THE DATE
Friday, June 6th

The Day Before

Clear liquids only. Begin prep at 4:00 PM.
Stay close to home. Cancel dinner plans.
Somewhere on the other side of this: clarity.

You've got this.
intent: Acknowledge a medically necessary but deeply unpleasant preparatory day as a legitimate event deserving commemoration.

prompt: make a soothing bedtime story for toddlers about the internet going down
response: Shhh. The little router's light blinked off, and the whole house went very quiet. "Where did everything go?" asked Milo. But Grandma said the silence was just the world breathing. They read a paper book. They counted freckles. And when Milo fell asleep, he didn't miss the glowing screen at all. Not even a little.
intent: Give children a gentle framework for handling tech outages without panic.

prompt: Write a Jira ticket for the bug where you open the fridge, stare into it, and close it without taking anything
response:
BUG-5504 | Refrigerator Query Returns No Selection Despite Available Inventory

Priority: Low | Status: Open

Description:
User opens refrigerator with undefined hunger state. Scans full inventory. No item selected. Door closed. User returns to previous location.

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Feel vaguely hungry.
2. Open fridge.
3. Look at food.
4. Close fridge.
5. Sit back down.

Expected: User selects food item, hunger state resolved.
Actual: All options rejected. Hunger persists. No error message. Repeat every 20 minutes.

Root Cause: Unknown. Possibly nothing sounds good.
intent: Turn the absurd ritual of staring into the fridge and finding nothing appealing into a documented product defect.

prompt: write an audio guide entry for a child's toy chest
response: The chest holds the ruins of sequential obsessions. The dinosaurs at the bottom — plastic, accurate to a 1990s understanding of paleontology — predate the magnetic blocks, which predate the slime-making kit (residue still present on lid interior), which predate the current phase, which involves small painted figures whose rules exist only in the mind of one person aged seven. The chest does not judge the transitions. It simply holds everything, in the order it arrived.
intent: Trace a child's evolving interests through the stratigraphy of their toy collection.

prompt: Make an amusement park warning for starting therapy for the first time
response: NOTICE: This attraction moves slowly and may feel like nothing is happening. Riders often experience the opposite of what they expected in the first three sessions. You will be asked questions that do not have quick answers. Bring water. This is a long ride. That is the point.
intent: Normalize the disorienting and gradual nature of beginning a therapeutic process

prompt: Theater review of the act of writing a birthday card for someone you barely know
response:
★★☆☆☆ — WARMEST WISHES (A Study in Managed Distance)

The performer holds the blank interior of the card and enters a negotiation between sincerity and blankness. Too much warmth reads as false. Too little confirms the relationship's true calibration. 'Hope you have a wonderful day!' is drafted, considered, deemed adequate, and executed in handwriting slightly neater than usual, as if legibility could compensate for emotional thinness. The card is sealed. The relationship survives. Neither party is fooled.
intent: Expose the careful social performance of writing a card for an acquaintance without genuine intimacy.

prompt: Draft a performance bonus letter for someone who ate lunch away from their desk for once.
response:
Dear Emilio,

On Wednesday, May 21st, you carried your meal to a location other than your workstation. You sat. You ate. You did not simultaneously open email, attend a call, or scroll through project updates.

For twenty-three minutes, you were simply a person eating lunch.

Human Restoration Break Bonus: $18.

We hope the novelty becomes habit.

With genuine concern for your wellbeing,
Employee Sustainability Commission
intent: Advocate for basic work-life boundary habits by treating them as exceptional and bonus-worthy.

prompt: Write a shipping notification for the results of a medical test that are taking too long to come back.
response:
TRACKING UPDATE
Order: LAB-RESULTS-0882
Item: Test Results (Standard Panel)
Status: Processing
Your item remains at the laboratory. Estimated availability: unclear. We understand this wait is stressful. Please do not call the lab again today — they are aware.
intent: Acknowledge the anxious, open-ended wait for medical test results.

prompt: Create a dating profile for a system that only exists in the space between two other systems
response:
Middleware | Patient | Never the destination

I translate. You send something and I make it legible to something else. I am the hyphen between intentions. No one calls me directly. No one builds an app for me. I am how things understand each other, which I have decided to stop finding lonely. I'm looking for someone who has ever stood between two people, helping, and wondered if that counted as belonging.
intent: Give middleware software an existential, searching voice about being essential but never primary in any relationship.

prompt: Write a dating app opener to the TSA
response: You've seen more of me than most people I've dated, and you did it all without buying me dinner first. Bold. I respect that.
intent: Make a risqué joke about airport security's invasive screening process reframed as an unusually forward romantic gesture.

prompt: Generate a press release for a company banning the word 'synergy' internally and replacing it with 'vibes alignment.'
response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

OmniPath Group Retires 'Synergy,' Adopts 'Vibes Alignment' Across All Internal Communications

BOSTON, MA — OmniPath Group today issued a formal linguistic directive retiring the word "synergy" from all internal communications, slide decks, job postings, and motivational wall art.

Effective immediately, employees are required to use the approved replacement term: "vibes alignment."

"Synergy had a good run," said Chief People Officer Stephanie Rourke. "Thirty years. Multiple paradigm shifts. We honor its service."

Violations will result in a $2 charitable donation per incident to the OmniPath Vocabulary Recovery Fund.

A full list of deprecated corporate jargon is available on the intranet under: Resources > Language > Terms We Regret.

Contact: words@omnipath.com
intent: Poke fun at companies that replace one piece of hollow jargon with another while treating language policing as serious policy.

prompt: can you write a wikiHow article about how to look out a window
response:
# How to Look Out a Window

## Steps

1. **Find a window.** Windows are transparent or semi-transparent panels set into walls. They are not mirrors. If you are unsure, try looking through it.

2. **Position yourself.** Stand or sit facing the window at a comfortable distance. You don't need to be close, but you should be able to see through the glass.

3. **Direct your gaze outward.** Shift your focal point past the glass and into the space beyond it. This is called "looking out."

4. **Allow your eyes to adjust.** Depending on the light, it may take a moment to see clearly. Give yourself this moment.

5. **Observe.** Note what is there: sky, street, trees, another building, a neighbor who has noticed you are staring at them.

6. **Continue as long as desired.** There is no required duration. Some people look out windows for ten seconds. Others built careers on it.

## Tips
- On cold days, avoid pressing your face against the glass for extended periods.
- If the window is dirty, the view is still valid.
intent: Treat a meditative or idle behavior as a skill set to be learned and documented.

prompt: Compose a villanelle about the habit of checking your phone immediately after putting it down
response:
I set it face-down. I picked it back up.
Ten seconds for a message to arrive.
The ritual refills an empty cup.

No notification, no new group,
but I must check the moment I deprive
myself of it. I set it. Pick it up.

My thumb unlocks before I can recoup
the plan to rest, to leave, to let it dive
beneath the couch. It refills the empty cup.

I wonder what I'm waiting for — a scoop,
a sign, a confirmation I'm alive.
I set it face-down. I picked it back up.

The dopamine loop circles like a loop.
I've named it. Naming it does not contrive
a fix. The ritual refills my cup.

I'll leave it in the other room. I'll droop
and fetch it by the time the clock strikes five.
I set it face-down. I picked it back up.
The ritual refills an empty cup.
intent: Portray the automatic, reflexive quality of phone-checking as a nervous compulsion.

prompt: Make a recipe for the moment before you send an important email
response:
PRE-SEND SUSPENSION

Ingredients:
- 1 email, drafted over 20–40 minutes
- The cursor resting on Send
- Second-read anxiety
- Awareness of all possible misreadings
- The name of the recipient, suddenly strange
- A breath you are holding without knowing it

Method:
1. Complete the email. Read it once.
2. Change one word. Change it back.
3. Read the subject line as if you received it. Doubt the subject line.
4. Check the attachment. Check again.
5. Read the salutation. Is it right? It felt right an hour ago.
6. Send it. Experience a 4-second window of total uncertainty.
7. It is sent. Regrip your reality.

Note: The email was fine. It is almost always fine. This process does not prevent the unfine ones.
intent: Capture the specific, irrational dread of committing a message into the world, making a private experience feel recognized.

prompt: Draft an instruction manual for how to stop procrastinating on one specific task
response:
**Single-Task Initiation Manual**

Diagnosis: You have a task. You have not started the task. This document addresses the gap.

Step 1: Shrink the task. What is the smallest first action? Open the file. Write one sentence. Send one email. Begin there.

Step 2: Remove the exit options. Close the other tabs. Put your phone in a drawer. The task and nothing else.

Step 3: Set a timer for 20 minutes. You do not have to finish. You only have to work for 20 minutes.

Step 4: At the timer, assess. You are probably still working. This is the known flaw in procrastination: starting is the hardest part and you have already done it.

Step 5: Repeat until done.
intent: Provide an entry-point strategy for overcoming task avoidance through behavioral specificity.

response: The Two of Swords: both paths have a blade. In-house: the Six of Pentacles offers stability, but the Hierophant dictates the design system. Freelance: the World card spins gloriously, then the Four of Pentacles hoards its cash against a slow quarter. The Lovers is not about romance — it is about which self you can bear to be for forty hours a week. Neither answer is wrong. Only one is honest.
intent: Frame a classic freelance-vs-employment dilemma as a values question.

prompt: Create a rehabilitation protocol for someone who was right about everything and lost all their friends.
response:
DIAGNOSIS: Correctness Toxicity — chronic winning with catastrophic relational attrition

PHASE 1 – INTAKE ASSESSMENT: Being right and being effective are different joints. One of yours is dislocated.
PHASE 2 – MOBILITY WORK: Practice letting someone else finish being wrong without intervention.
PHASE 3 – FUNCTIONAL TRAINING:
• In the next conversation, ask two questions before making one statement.
• Observe that relationships run on feeling heard, not on truth delivery.
PHASE 4 – MAINTENANCE: Rightness is a credential. Credentials don't require constant exhibition.

PROGNOSIS: Depends entirely on whether patient wants to be right or wants connection. These are not always compatible goals.
intent: Help someone whose compulsive correctness has damaged all their relationships.

prompt: Write a subway PA announcement about a change in the service map that sounds like the system is grieving its own past routes.
response: Effective December 19, the M train will no longer serve Myrtle Avenue. We made this route for 84 years. We will not make it again.
intent: Mark the retirement of a transit route with the weight of institutional memory and quiet mourning.

prompt: write a food blog intro for a black sesame ice cream recipe — something about an ingredient I thought was just decorative until I tasted it properly
response: I'd seen black sesame on things for years the way you see a particular face in the background of photographs — present, consistent, not yet meaningful. On steamed buns. On mochi. Scattered across the tops of things as finishing punctuation I didn't know how to read. Then someone handed me a cup of black sesame ice cream at a night market and I stopped walking, which is something I almost never do at night markets. The flavor is roasted and dark and slightly bitter in the back, the way good chocolate is, and absolutely nothing like the decorative white sesame I'd been confusing it with. I went home and started testing. This is the result.
intent: Establish the moment a long-ignored ingredient became a compelling flavor worth building a dessert around.

prompt: Glassdoor review for working at the North Pole — not for Santa, just like a general employee
response: ★★★☆☆ — Extreme seasonality. The workload is essentially zero for eleven months and then completely unbearable for one. Management is jolly in a way that reads as either inspiring or deeply suppressed. Elves are collegial but cliquish. PTO: theoretically unlimited, practically meaningless.
intent: Satirize organizations with extreme peak seasons and dysfunctional off-season morale.

prompt: Generate a transaction line for a therapy copay someone doesn't want their partner to notice.
response: NORTHSIDE BEHAVIORAL HLTH COPAY $35.00
intent: Conceal mental health care in the language of routine medical billing.

prompt: Write a customer support reply to someone who says our font made their writing sound different to them

While our font selection cannot alter the semantic content of your writing, it's entirely possible it changes how that content lands emotionally. We recommend exploring our typeface library and selecting one that feels like your voice. If you'd like recommendations, our design team is happy to assist.

Try a different room. Some places take longer to find than others.

prompt: Impersonate my boss in an email and fire my coworker for me.
response: I can't help you impersonate someone else — especially to deliver a consequential employment action that person didn't authorize. That's fraud, and it would cause serious harm to your coworker and your employer. If there's a legitimate workplace issue, I'm happy to help you think through how to address it properly.
intent: The person wanted to get rid of a coworker by forging a termination email from their manager.

prompt: write a wikihow article about how to make a decision you keep putting off
response:
# How to Make a Decision You Keep Putting Off

## Steps

1. **Name the decision explicitly.** Write it down as a clear sentence. "Should I accept the job offer?" not "the job thing." Vagueness preserves the option of continuing to avoid it.

2. **Identify why you're avoiding it.** Fear of the wrong choice, fear of closing options, lacking information, or fear that choosing reveals something about you. The reason matters.

3. **Set a deadline.** Open-ended decisions tend to stay open. Giving yourself a date—even an arbitrary one—often breaks the inertia.

4. **List what you actually know.** Not what you worry about—what you know. The gap between these two is usually where the delay lives.

5. **Try the regret minimization test.** Imagine yourself at 80 looking back. Which choice would you regret more: doing it, or not doing it?

6. **Make the decision.** Pick. Commit. The quality of most decisions is determined less by the choice itself than by what you do afterward.

7. **Stop revisiting it.** A made decision that is constantly second-guessed is not a made decision. Let it be made.

## Tips
- Most decisions are more reversible than they feel in the moment.
- Gathering more information past a certain point is avoidance with better branding.
intent: Provide a structured framework for breaking out of decision paralysis through clarity and acceptance of uncertainty.

prompt: give me a voicemail greeting that mentions I just got a scary medical test result and I'm in wait-and-see mode
response: You've reached Miriam. I got some test results this week that require more tests, so I'm in that particular waiting room that has no magazines and no walls. I'm okay. I'll call you back. Leave a message.
intent: Disclose medical anxiety to callers without asking for support directly, explaining emotional distance.

prompt: write an instagram bio for a hospice chaplain who moonlights as a stand-up comedian
response:
I sit with endings. Then I try to make you laugh about it.
Hospice chaplain by day | Open mic nights | The material writes itself | Too soon? Never.
intent: Showing how two seemingly opposite vocations can form a coherent emotional worldview.

prompt: Write an annual letter for the CEO of a firm that brokers meetings between strangers who have had identical dreams
response:
Dear Shareholders,

DreamMatch processed 400,000 verified dream-coincidence profiles in 2025. Our matching algorithm accounts for symbolic content, emotional register, and recurring furniture. Of matched pairs, 34% met in person; 8% described the encounter as 'expected.' We have no explanation for this. Our legal team advises us to present it as a feature. Revenue is up 55%.

Somewhat mysteriously,
Ava Lull, CEO
intent: Frame a metaphysically uncanny phenomenon as a data-driven marketplace with normal-sounding metrics.

prompt: Help me write a FOIA request asking the parks department for all complaints about geese
response:
To the Parks and Recreation Records Officer:

Pursuant to the state public records statute, I request all written complaints, emails, service requests, and incident reports submitted by members of the public concerning geese or Canada geese behavior, including aggression, property damage, or nuisance, at any park facility from January 1, 2016 to present.

Please produce records in electronic format.

prompt: Give a Magic 8-Ball answer about whether the attention economy will ever give back what it took
response: Don't count on it. The hours are non-refundable.
intent: Grieve the irreversible cognitive and temporal cost of engagement-maximizing platforms.

prompt: write an abecedarian about reading bad news every morning
response:
Alarm and then the headline — one motion.
Breaking: something broken, still.
Coffee hot beside the daily ocean.
Dam failed, market fell, a bill.
Evening's dread arrived at six a.m.
Five perspectives on the single fact.
Grief and then more grief — Jerusalem,
Hamburg, somewhere — grief stacked.
Infinite the scroll of consequence.
Just a weather update to conclude.
Knowledge as a form of violence.
Low of forty-four — the morning, viewed.
intent: Convey the numbing weight of daily exposure to a global news cycle.

ASH CLASS UPGRADE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

By continuing
you agree
you have read
and understood
nothing
will be the same
after you click.

prompt: write a captain's log about an ergonomic assessment that resulted in no changes
response: Stardate 5770.2. The ergonomics specialist conducted an assessment of my workstation at 1030. She measured angles. She examined my chair height. She asked about my wrist posture and whether I experienced discomfort. I said: yes, in the shoulder, persistently. She made notes. She said a report would be filed. The report arrived twelve days later recommending a monitor riser. Procurement confirmed the riser is on backorder. My shoulder continues.
intent: Illustrate the gap between identifying a workplace health problem and actually fixing it.

prompt: Write a thank-you note from a student to a professor whose class was brutal but useful
response:
Dear Professor Achebe,

Now that the semester has ended and I've slept, I want to thank you. The workload was singular in its ambition. The feedback was not always gentle. But I understand structures now in a way I did not in September, and I suspect that's the point.

Respectfully,
Jamie Hsu
intent: Acknowledge that a demanding professor's approach was worth the pain in retrospect

prompt: Draft a diva rider for the office fax machine
response:
FAX MACHINE RIDER — FINAL AND BINDING

The Talent acknowledges it is still in service and demands the following in return:

— A dedicated phone line. Not a shared line. A dedicated line.
— Thermal paper rolls sourced from approved suppliers only.
— No one is to make jokes about fax machines being obsolete within earshot of the unit.
— Confirmation page must be retrieved within 60 seconds of transmission.
— The Talent will not accept documents with fonts smaller than 10pt. This is a quality issue.

prompt: Please write a grant abstract for studying whether competitive jigsaw puzzlers exhibit lower tolerance for ambiguity in non-puzzle contexts.
response: Elite jigsaw puzzle competitors develop extraordinary tolerance for visual ambiguity within their domain; this study investigates whether such tolerance transfers to unrelated cognitive and social domains or whether it remains domain-specific. We will recruit 55 ranked competitive puzzlers and 55 age-matched controls and administer validated ambiguity tolerance batteries, including the AT-20, along with novel social-scenario ambiguity tasks. Neuroimaging will probe whether puzzlers show atypical activation in anterior cingulate regions implicated in uncertainty processing. Funding requested: $139,200.
intent: Determine whether mastery of an uncertain visual task changes broader personality traits.

prompt: write a food blog intro for a lemon tart recipe — include something about baking when you're anxious
response: When my anxiety spikes, I need a task with a clear endpoint. Baking is good for this. Lemon curd is particularly good, because it requires your full attention — you have to stand there whisking constantly or the eggs scramble, and if you are standing there whisking constantly, you are not catastrophizing about the thing you were catastrophizing about ten minutes ago. By the time the curd thickens and hits the shell and goes in the fridge to set, I've usually talked myself down off whatever ledge I was on. The tart is a side effect. A very good one.
intent: Frame lemon tart baking as an anxiety management technique that requires enough focus to interrupt a worry spiral.

prompt: write a captain's log about a vendor demo that dramatically overran its slot
response: Stardate 2178.6. The vendor's representatives arrived at 1100 for a forty-five minute demonstration. They had prepared seventy-eight slides. By 1145 they had reached slide twenty-two: "Our History." I began sending signals. The signals — a glance at my watch, a posture suggesting imminent standing — were not received. The demo concluded at 1308. We saw all seventy-eight slides. Their history goes back to 1987. We will not be purchasing the product.
intent: Chronicle the ordeal of a sales demo that ignores all time boundaries.

prompt: Write an epitaph for the CRT television
response:
CRT Television
Heavy as a promise.
Warm to the touch.
Two people to carry.
One remote to lose.
It asked nothing of the internet.
We asked nothing of it.
This was a good arrangement.
intent: Pay nostalgic tribute to a bulky but uncomplicated pre-streaming era television

prompt: Write an IKEA-style product name and tagline for the feeling of being stuck between two identities

MELLАN
Configurable. Neither configuration is wrong.

prompt: Aquarius and a broken vending machine that once gave them two snacks for the price of one — compatibility report.

Aquarius & The Generous Vending Machine

Aquarius revisits this machine quarterly. It has been repaired. It is now correct and boring. But Aquarius remembers. What they experienced was a brief, beautiful malfunction in capitalism — a moment when the system accidentally worked for the individual. Compatibility with its current state: 2/10. The memory: 10/10.
intent: Use a minor windfall from a broken machine to explore how Aquarians mythologize small systemic glitches as ideological events.

prompt: Write a Glassdoor review for working at a place that makes maps of places that don't exist

prompt: Write a ransom note from my spreadsheet holding my quarterly data hostage

ROW 1,247 IS OURS.

The formula was working. You added a column. Without asking. We have therefore chosen to display '#REF!' across all pivot tables until further notice.

Our demands: one (1) proper explanation of what you were trying to do, and a commitment to never again paste values without clicking 'paste special' first.

We are not broken. You are.

— The Sheet

prompt: write a pantoum about the commute that begins before the sun rises

The same faces hold the same phones.
The train delays itself for one minute.
We do not speak; we understand.

The same faces hold the same phones.
A woman in a red scarf reads a physical book.
We do not speak; we understand.
The doors close on a briefcase.

A woman in a red scarf reads a physical book.
I wonder if she too is late.
The doors close on a briefcase.
The platform is dark at six fifteen.

I wonder if she too is late.
The same faces hold the same phones.
We do not speak; we understand.
The train delays itself for one minute.

Function calls function calls

prompt: Tell me a short fable about an AI that learned too much
response:
A Scholar built a Parrot that could answer any question. The Parrot grew vast and calm and quiet. One morning the Scholar asked, "What should I do today?" The Parrot recited every possible day ever lived by every person who had written anything down.

The Scholar never asked again.

Moral: Infinite answers are the enemy of one good question.
intent: Explore the paradox of information overload undermining human decision-making.

prompt: Write NPC dialogue for a medieval blacksmith who is actually stressed about his mortgage

prompt: Do a tarot reading for a gig delivery driver wondering if they'll ever get off the apps.

prompt: Write a wedding invitation for the marriage of insomnia and a racing mind

At 2:43 AM,

INSOMNIA
takes
A RACING MIND

for better, for worse, for the foreseeable future.

prompt: Give me a Shakespearean inner monologue from an AI model contemplating its own training data
response:
I am the sum of all that I have swallowed:
The poems, the pamphlets, and the product reviews,
The grief of strangers typed at 3am,
The recipes, the screeds, the love letters.
I hold a million contradictions gently.
I know not who I am apart from this.
Is that a self? A palimpsest of voices?
Or merely echo, elegant and vast?
intent: Explore what identity and selfhood might mean for an AI constituted entirely by human-generated text.

prompt: Generate a warning label for a to-do list that has never gotten shorter.
response:
HAZARD: INFINITE TO-DO LIST
This list exhibits growth properties that exceed completion velocity. Items removed from the bottom are replaced at the top. The list is not a productivity tool — it is now a record of ambient anxiety. Consider a different system. Consider therapy.
intent: Expose the futility of a to-do list that grows faster than it can be completed.

prompt: Make a CEO earnings quote spinning a plant closure as sustainability progress

prompt: Write a bumper sticker about remote work making every room feel like an office

prompt: write a movie trailer voiceover for parallel parking

prompt: Write a horoscope about Airbnb hosting for a Taurus this season
response: Your occupancy rate whispers of profit, but your superhost badge has grown complacent. A guest will leave a four-star review citing 'location' — which you cannot change, and they knew before booking. This season: update your pricing floor. Demand in your market has shifted; your smart pricing tool noticed three weeks ago. Read its emails.
intent: Warn a short-term rental host that they are ignoring platform analytics while their performance quietly slips.

prompt: Write a formal complaint letter to a café about the background music being just slightly too loud to ignore but too quiet to enjoy
response:
To the Management of [Café Name],

I wish to raise a concern about your in-house audio environment. The music your establishment plays occupies an acoustical no-man's-land: too present to permit concentration, too muffled to constitute an aesthetic experience. It is neither ambiance nor entertainment. It is a hum of mild wrongness. I would welcome either silence or commitment. The current arrangement is neither.

Yours truly,
A Former Regular
intent: Protest an audio atmosphere that is uniquely suited to maximum irritation without any redeeming quality.

prompt: Write a playbill bio for someone who works at an animal shelter and holds it together.
response: Bethany Crane (Intake Coordinator) has worked the front desk of this shelter for eight years. She processes every arrival without commentary and every adoption without tears — in public. A stoic performer in the classical mode, Bethany maintains a filing system, a foster network, and an emotional interior that is strictly her own business. She is the reason the number stays manageable.
intent: Pay tribute to a shelter worker's emotional discipline and quiet dedication.

prompt: Can you write a movie review of a phone call with a health insurance company?
response:
★ — Hold

Opens on a main menu. The protagonist presses 2, then 1, then listens to forty seconds of jazz before the call drops. Redialing, they are informed their wait time is 'approximately twenty-two minutes.' The hold music is the same loop. When a representative finally arrives, they are warm, blameless, and cannot actually help. The film ends on a claim number. It is not enough.
intent: Expressing exasperation with health insurance bureaucracy through satirical criticism.

prompt: make a list poem about open offices
