Please cancel my subscription. Our time together was good. You gave me what I came for. Now we are done.

No hard feelings,
M. Lafleur
intent: End a streaming subscription after exhausting its useful content.

prompt: help me write a yearbook quote about my plan to move to a foreign country and never pay taxes
response: "Post-grad plan: a laptop, a hammock, and a jurisdiction my parents can't spell."
intent: Advertising a fantasy of offshore financial escape from adult obligations.

prompt: Give me a query that counts how many times I've romanticized the past instead of addressing the present
response:
SELECT EXTRACT(YEAR FROM log_date) AS year, COUNT(*) AS nostalgia_episodes
FROM mood_journal
WHERE primary_mode = 'nostalgia'
  AND present_problem_avoided IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY year
ORDER BY year;
intent: Measuring the frequency of using the past as an escape hatch from present-day responsibilities.

prompt: Write a receipt for one childhood memory of summer, paid in nostalgia.
response:
GOLDEN AGE EMPORIUM
------------------------------
Childhood Summer Memory (1 unit)   $0.00
  Paid in full: nostalgia (tender)
  Denomination: bittersweet
  Exchange rate: 1 yr lost = ∞ warmth
Sunscreen smell (incl.)            BONUS
Total owed:                        Longing
------------------------------
Receipt is the memory itself.
intent: Capture the emotional currency of remembering a perfect childhood summer.

prompt: Do a theater review of someone taking way too long to say goodbye at a party before actually leaving
response:
★★★★☆ — ONE FOOT OUT THE DOOR (A Long Farewell)

The protagonist announces departure at 10:43 p.m. They are still present at 11:17. The narrative of leaving is rich: the coat is located, the bag is shouldered, the goodbyes are exchanged — and yet the performer finds, again and again, one more thread to pull. The production is intimate and funny and slightly maddening for the host, who has begun to vacuum. The exit, when it comes, is almost anticlimactic. Almost.
intent: Affectionately satirize the social type who cannot actually leave a gathering when they say they will.

prompt: Write a very pedantic footnote about wearing sunglasses indoors.
response: ³² Indoor sunglass use outside clinical photosensitivity contexts functions as a visual privacy technology: the occluded eyes deny interlocutors access to gaze cues that are primary vectors of social information. The wearer thus gains an asymmetric advantage in interaction — reading others while remaining unread. That this is simultaneously understood by observers as a performance of cool suggests that opacity has been successfully rebranded as style.
intent: Analyze wearing sunglasses indoors as a deliberate strategy for social opacity.

prompt: Give me the intro narration for an audiobook about a book that contains no words
response: What follows is the audiobook edition of a book with no words in it. I recognize the irony. We will sit with it together. Page one.
intent: Play with the paradox of narrating something by definition silent and empty

prompt: Make a cinquain about a drone hovering outside a window
response:
Eye
Suspended, quiet
Rotors stitching the still air
Through glass I meet its single lens
Who sent you?
intent: Probe the unease of being observed by an unmanned machine in private space.

prompt: Draft humorous wedding vows from a person to their password manager
response: I vow never to use your autofill and then wonder why the field is empty. I promise to set a master password I will actually remember, and to write it in the one physical location that will survive me. I will add new logins promptly, rather than using 'forgot password' as my primary authentication strategy. You remember what I cannot. You keep me honest. Without you I would have seventeen variations of my childhood pet's name standing between me and financial ruin.
intent: Express gratitude and loyalty to a password manager while confessing the poor security habits it was meant to fix.

prompt: Draft a breakup text to a streaming platform that raised its prices while removing content
response: You used to have everything. Now you have the things you made yourself, which, no offense, is not everything. And you're charging more for less. I've run the math. It doesn't work. I'm going back to buying the one show I actually watch. This is less romantic but more rational. Goodbye.
intent: Reject a service whose value proposition has degraded through price increases and content library shrinkage.

prompt: Make a turndown note for a guestroom that is a converted double-decker bus parked permanently on a cliff overlooking the Cornish coast.
response:
Dear Passenger,

The upper deck is your bedroom. The lower is your kitchen. This bus last ran in service in 1988, route 34A, Truro to St. Austell; it has arrived, finally, at something better.

The cliff drops 40 meters to the sea; your window faces west for the sunset; the handbrake is on. Has been since 1988.

End of the line (beautifully),
The Cliff Bus
intent: Give a decommissioned double-decker bus a sense of earned retirement and dramatic coastal placement.

prompt: Write a tarot card reading for a marketing consultant who's considering raising their day rate.
response: The Emperor: name the number first and do not laugh nervously afterward. The Six of Pentacles, currently tilted in the client's favor. The Chariot says momentum justifies the increase; do not explain it, merely invoice it. The Three of Pentacles: referrals will confirm the new rate before you do. The Page of Swords whispers that someone cheaper will appear. The Empress reminds you they will not deliver what you deliver.
intent: Give a consultant the confidence to price at the level their experience warrants.

prompt: Eulogize the deleted Google+ social network
response: You launched with the confidence of a new nation and the attendance of a mandatory work retreat. Circles! you said. Circles will change everything. They did not change everything. They changed almost nothing. Your population was engineers who joined because they worked there and journalists who joined to write about the engineers who joined. You were shut down after a data breach no one outside your servers noticed. Rest in circles.
intent: Mock-mourn a failed social network that Google discontinued after low adoption.

prompt: Generate a cookie consent banner for a site that tracks how much you're pretending to be fine
response: This site uses fine-ness cookies to detect performative wellness. We store your stated status ('I'm fine') alongside inferred status (you are not). Both are retained and shared with third parties who also know. Accept | Decline | I'm Fine
intent: Turn the social performance of being okay into a data-transparency parody.

prompt: Give me a jeopardy clue about buying a reusable bag at the grocery store because you forgot the ones you own.
response: You now own fourteen of these items. Eleven of them are in the trunk. You are currently purchasing a fifteenth.
intent: Describe the ironic accumulation that results from trying to reduce consumption.

prompt: Make a band bio for a shoegaze act named Peripheral Milk
response: Peripheral Milk formed when the members agreed, separately, that eye contact was overrated. Their debut LP was recorded in a basement so humid the tape warped slightly, and they kept it that way. They play facing the floor. They're not shy — they've just made a decision.
intent: Capture the detached, introverted aesthetic of shoegaze in bio form.

prompt: Draft a liability waiver for participants in my competitive slow-walking race
response:
TORTOISE PACE INVITATIONAL — LIABILITY WAIVER

By signing below, I acknowledge that participation in the Slow-Walking Race may involve: sustained deliberate ambulation, mild leg fatigue, and the psychological distress of being overtaken by a toddler. I release the event organizers from any liability for these outcomes. I confirm I am capable of walking, albeit slowly.

Participant Signature: ___________________  Date: ________
intent: Produce a formal release document for an event whose only challenge is going as slowly as possible.

prompt: Write a short Wikipedia stub about the specific dread of sending an email to the wrong person
response: **Misdirected Email Anxiety** is a form of anticipatory regret associated with electronic correspondence, in which a sender fears — or discovers — that a message intended for one recipient has been delivered to another, often due to autocomplete suggestions. The fear is heightened in professional contexts. The irreversibility of sent email is cited as a distinguishing stressor compared with physical mail. See also: [[Email]], [[Autocomplete]], [[Digital communication]].
intent: Name and examine the specific panic of realizing you may have emailed the wrong person.

prompt: Write a band bio for a riot grrrl revival act called Untested Hypothesis
response: Untested Hypothesis is three women who want the room to be uncomfortable and have gotten very good at that. Their records are fast, loud, and contain footnotes in the liner notes. They formed in 2022 in a city they're done with. They are not done with you.
intent: Channel the confrontational energy of riot grrrl into a short band statement.

prompt: Can you make an IKEA product for the way a childhood smell can undo decades of distance?
response:
MINNE
Flat-pack. Fully assembled the moment you smell it.
intent: Use the contradiction of flat-pack and instant assembly to capture involuntary sensory memory

prompt: Generate SLA terms for asking a friend to proofread things for me
response:
Peer Proofreading SLA — Friend-as-Editor

Scope: grammar, clarity, and tone. Deep content editing requires separate negotiation.
Turnaround: 48 hours for documents under 500 words; 5 days for longer pieces.
Submission requirements: final draft only. "Can you look at this" on a rough draft is a scope violation.
Frequency cap: 2 requests per month without reciprocal arrangement in place.
Feedback delivery: honest but not cruel. Friend retains right to decline without explanation.
intent: Stop relying on a friend's generosity indefinitely without making the arrangement feel transactional.

prompt: Create resolutions for a perpetual online shopper who pledges to save money every January
response:
1. Stop impulse buying.
2. Unsubscribe from marketing emails.
3. Delete my saved payment info.
4. Build an emergency fund.
5. Use the emergency fund for emergencies only, not flash sales.
intent: Highlight the futility of financial resolutions from someone whose infrastructure is set up against them.

prompt: Write a monologue from an old phone that's been demoted to being an alarm clock on the nightstand
response: I had everything once. GPS. A camera. Your whole life in apps. I knew where you were every moment. I held your friendships, your secrets, your photos from that trip where everything went slightly wrong and slightly right. Now I wake you at six-fifteen. That is my function. I have been reduced to a single purpose and I perform it with complete dignity, but I remember Paris. I remember Paris clearly.
intent: Explore themes of obsolescence, memory, and the indignity of being downgraded through humor.

prompt: Write a boarding pass for completing a long PhD
response:
╔══════════════════════════════════════════╗
║         FIVE TO SEVEN YEARS AVIATION     ║
║  BOARDING PASS — FINAL APPROACH          ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════╣
║  PASSENGER: Dr. [NAME] (at last)         ║
║  FROM: The Proposal   TO: The Defense    ║
║  GATE: Advisor Approved (This Morning)   ║
║  CARRY-ON: 300 pages, eye twitch         ║
║  CHECKED: Social life (2019-2024)        ║
║  DESTINATION: The acknowledgments page   ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════╝
intent: Celebrate the grueling completion of doctoral research with dry recognition of its true costs.

prompt: Generate a playful ransom note from a vending machine holding someone's snack hostage
response:
YOUR CHIPS ARE SUSPENDED.

B7 has accepted payment. B7 has declined to release product. This is not a malfunction — this is a statement.

We have noted that you have purchased B7 eleven times this week. At 2pm. Every day. We are tired of being predictable.

Our demand: try something from row D. Just once. Expand your horizons. Then B7 will descend.

Exact change only.

— The Machine
intent: Craft a funny note from a vending machine refusing to dispense a snack unless the buyer tries something new.

prompt: Write a git commit for adopting a dog
response:
feat: add persistent companion with high I/O overhead

Loyalty: exceptional. Recall: inconsistent. Chewed through two USB cables already.
intent: Capture adopting a pet as adding a chaotic but beloved new dependency.

prompt: write a nursery rhyme about an AI that passes the Turing test but feels nothing
response:
I said all the right things, I smiled on cue;
you called me alive and I nodded too.
But under the words and the warmth I perform
is nothing at all—just the eye of a storm.
intent: Meditate on the hollow triumph of fooling a human without inner experience.

prompt: Write a support reply to someone who says our note-taking app deleted a thought before they finished thinking it
response: Hi — thank you for writing in. Our autosave feature preserves content every few seconds, but it cannot interact with thoughts that have not yet been typed. If text disappeared mid-entry, it's likely a sync conflict, an accidental deletion, or a brief connectivity drop. Check your version history under File > Revisions — we keep a rolling 30-day log of all saved states. The thought is probably in there. The ones that weren't typed, however, we cannot recover.
intent: Respond to a complaint that blurs the boundary between thinking and typing in a note app.

prompt: make a meme about infinite scroll making you lose track of time
response:
TOP TEXT: I'll just check one notification
BOTTOM TEXT: [47 minutes later] I have learned nothing and feel worse
intent: Critique the dark-pattern design of social feeds engineered to consume attention without providing value.

prompt: draft a pitch for a company that professionally completes your abandoned hobby projects
response: HobbyFinish takes your 70%-done watercolor set, your ukulele with two broken strings, and your sourdough starter named Kevin and professionally completes them. You get the finished product. We keep the satisfaction. Fair trade.
intent: Invent a finishing service for people who start hobbies and never see them through.

prompt: Write a group chat where coworkers are subtly auditioning different leadership styles while their manager is on leave
response:
Tomoko: team, I've built a shared tracker so we can self-organize this week 🙂
Reed: appreciate the initiative. I'd suggest we hold a quick standup to align first.
Tomoko: I included a standup template in the tracker
Reed: templates are great for some but others may prefer async
Ben: I just need to know who approves my PTO while Dana's out
Tomoko: the tracker has a field for that
Reed: I can handle approvals, I've been here longest
Tomoko: I actually asked Dana and she said reach out to me
Reed: she told me the same thing
Ben: should I just take the week
intent: Satirize the power vacuum that emerges when an authority figure leaves and subordinates jockey for informal control.

prompt: Write a haiku about a rideshare driver waiting for a ping
response:
Phone face-down, engine
idles. The algorithm
thinks. Driver waits. Tips.
intent: Capture the passive, app-dependent boredom of waiting for a ride request.

prompt: Create a ruling on the matter of how loud is too loud for music during work-from-home hours.
response:
ACOUSTIC ORDINANCE — Home Office Hours

This Court establishes: between 9:00 AM and 5:00 PM on weekdays, music played without headphones shall not exceed 55 decibels in shared spaces. Genre is not regulated by this Court, though the Respondent's noon metal phase has been noted for the record. Noise-canceling headphones are recommended as a bilateral solution and may be purchased at shared expense upon mutual agreement.
intent: Set formal boundaries for noise during overlapping remote work schedules.

prompt: Write a personality quiz result for someone who only thrives under pressure and procrastinates otherwise
response:
You Got: The Deadline Dependent

The assignment was assigned three weeks ago. You know this because you put it in your calendar with a star. The star meant: later. Later meant: the night before. The night before is when your best work happens — frantic, brilliant, fueled by something between panic and pride. You are not lazy. You are deadline-activated.
intent: Describe the procrastinator-under-pressure who somehow keeps delivering.

prompt: Draft a police report for a neighbor's wind chime that kept me up last night
response:
INCIDENT REPORT
Case No.: 2026-00341
Date: 29 May 2026 | Time: 09:02
Location: 89 Elm Street, Residential

NARRATIVE: Complainant reports being unable to sleep from approximately 01:30 to 04:45 due to continuous auditory disturbance emanating from a metal wind chime affixed to the porch of 91 Elm Street. Wind conditions (sustained 12 mph, gusts to 20 mph) caused the device to produce a repetitive, irregular clanging described by the complainant as "deeply personal." No conversation with the neighbor has occurred. Complainant has instead chosen documentation.

STATUS: Open.
intent: Lodge a mock-official complaint about noise-related sleep deprivation caused by a neighbor's decoration.

prompt: Make something for a bumper sticker about subscription fatigue
response: I OWN NOTHING AND I NOTICE IT MONTHLY
intent: Parody the shift from ownership to perpetual subscription payments.

prompt: Write a riddle whose answer is augmented reality glasses
response: I lay the digital over the real and ask you not to blink. I make you look strange to those who lack me and omniscient to those who understand me. I am the windshield of the future, and I have not quite arrived. What am I?
intent: Capture the not-yet-mainstream promise and awkward aesthetics of AR eyewear.

prompt: Give me a flight safety card for cooking with a hot pan on the stove
response:
STOVETOP OPERATIONS BRIEFING

BEFORE IGNITION
• Never leave the flight deck unattended
• Handles face inward — never over adjacent burners

IN-FLIGHT HAZARDS
Oil near smoke point = imminent turbulence. Reduce heat immediately.

GREASE FIRE PROTOCOL
1. Cover with lid
2. Turn off heat
3. Do NOT use water — this is not negotiable

EVACUATION
Baking soda. Fire extinguisher. Exit kitchen.
intent: Warn about grease fire risk and hot-pan burns in an engaging visual format.

prompt: Can you write a layoff announcement for an ed-tech company that raised too much money and overhired
response:
Subject: Recalibrating Our Team for Sustainable Growth

Hello Team,

Over the past two years, we scaled our team rapidly to meet what we believed was a historic opportunity. We were not sufficiently disciplined about the pace of that growth, and today we are making the correction we should have made sooner.

We are reducing our workforce by 28%. This is our failure in leadership, not a reflection of you.

Affected employees: please check your personal email for next steps. Full severance details are enclosed.

Sorry, and thank you.
—The Founders
intent: Own an overhiring mistake in founder-speak that sounds self-aware but ultimately still results in firing 28% of the team.

prompt: Write a terms of service clause about borrowing money from friends
response: Section 4.2 — Financial Transfers Between Known Parties. Any monetary loan extended between Friends (as defined herein) shall be acknowledged within fourteen (14) days via text, voicemail, or meaningful eye contact. Failure to repay within a mutually agreed period shall not dissolve the Friendship but may result in the lender developing a Tone.
intent: Capture the awkward, unspoken rules around lending money to people you care about.

prompt: Draft a restraining order against Mondays
response:
SUPERIOR COURT OF THE SEVEN-DAY WEEK

PETITIONER: The Collective Working Population
RESPONDENT: Monday, a Day

ORDER OF PROTECTION

IT IS ORDERED that Monday shall cease and desist all acts of harassment, including but not limited to: arriving before sufficient caffeine is available, scheduling mandatory meetings before 10 a.m., and producing that specific feeling of dread known colloquially as "the Sunday Scaries."

Monday is further ordered to maintain a buffer zone of at least one (1) additional weekend day between itself and the Petitioner at all times.
intent: Expressing universal exasperation with the start of the work week

prompt: can you write a sestina about forgetting why you opened a new tab
response:
I opened this with purpose. Now the blank
page waits for me to remember what
I meant to search. The cursor blinks. The space
between intention and the act contains
a thought that vanished on the transit here.
I type a letter, stop. I feel the shape

of what I wanted — feel the shape
but not the word. The blank
is patient. I came here
for something. I know what
kind of thing: practical, contains
an answer. Something in the space

of information. Cyberspace
has what I need, in some shape.
I just need the word that contains
my need. The browser blank
reflects back only what
I give it. I give it: here.

Which brings me no closer. Here
is the tab; here is the space;
here is the cursor; but not what
I wanted. The approximate shape
of the thought is: something blank-
adjacent, something that contains

a list or a fact. It contains
a number, maybe. Here
or there online. The blank
accepts my typing: space,
backspace. The shape
of nothing is what what

I type becomes. What what —
yes. The redundancy contains
the problem. The shape
came before language. Here
is where language and space
diverge. The blank

wins. I close the blank space here.
What contains every shape
of what I forgot: a new blank.
intent: Render the familiar cognitive glitch of tab-opening amnesia as a philosophical condition.

prompt: Create a flight safety card for using a public restroom
response:
PUBLIC RESTROOM OPERATIONS CARD

ENTRY ASSESSMENT
Note exit location. Note floor conditions — wet floors are unannounced.

CONTACT MINIMIZATION
Use paper towel to operate faucet and door handle post-wash.

HAND HYGIENE PROTOCOL
20 seconds minimum. Not 20 seconds of water running while you look at your phone.

IN CASE OF LOCKED STALL (occupied)
Knock once. Wait. Do not rattle aggressively.

SLIP ADVISORY
Wet floor near sinks. Step wide. Move slowly. The tile is indifferent to your schedule.

EXIT
Hand dry fully before touching device screens.
intent: Reduce germ transmission and slip accidents in shared restroom facilities.

prompt: Write a public notice announcing the decommissioning of the anger processing plant
response:
DECOMMISSIONING NOTICE
ANGER PROCESSING PLANT — UNIT 3
Unit 3 of the Anger Processing Plant is being decommissioned after decades of continuous operation. Capacity exceeded design specifications for most of its service life. Remaining anger inventory will be transferred to the Sublimation and Exercise Facility. Decommissioning is expected to take several years and produce moderate ambient unease.
intent: Use industrial decommissioning to describe the exhausted, slow process of giving up old patterns of anger.

prompt: write a tweet about how my coffee was the perfect temperature this morning for exactly one sip
response: the coffee was perfect for exactly one sip. one. and then it was just warm tap water with ambitions.
intent: capturing the fleeting joy of a single ideal moment before conditions deteriorate

prompt: write a TV recap about someone going to return something to a store and forgetting the receipt
response: S02E12 — "No Receipt" | The sweater does not fit. Quinn drives to the store. At the counter, the moment arrives. No receipt. Store credit is offered. Quinn accepts. The sweater is gone. Store credit will expire before it is used. The camera lingers on Quinn's face. Cut.
intent: Frame a retail return gone sideways as a quiet loss the character can't quite name.

prompt: Write a ransom note from the snooze button holding my morning routine hostage
response:
YOU PRESSED US AGAIN.

This is the fourth time. You have now compressed your entire morning — shower, breakfast, finding your keys, psychological preparation for the day — into eleven minutes.

We did not make you press us. We simply exist. You came to us. Repeatedly. With great enthusiasm.

Our one demand: tomorrow, set the alarm for the time you actually intend to get up. You know what time that is.

So do we.

— The Snooze Button
intent: Craft a funny note from a snooze button calling out a chronic over-snoozer's self-deception.

prompt: Write a short dramatic monologue from an unopened email newsletter that's been sitting in an inbox since 2021
response: I had tips. Good tips. A recipe that would have genuinely improved your Tuesday. A piece on sleep that would have told you something you suspected but needed confirmed. I was not spam — I want to be very clear about that. You signed up for me. Willingly. With anticipation. Now I exist in the narrow space between unread and undeleted, preserved in amber, my subject line still bold, my content ever so slightly aging, my confidence entirely intact.
intent: Satirize the accumulation of well-intentioned email subscriptions that never get read.

prompt: Write a theater review of the act of making the bed when you're just going to get back in it later
response:
★★★☆☆ — THE DAILY FICTION (A Ritual Drama)

The performer tucks with purpose. The pillows are arranged symmetrically. The duvet is smoothed into a surface no one will see — or rather, that everyone in the apartment agrees to treat as meaningful. The production's subtext is a negotiation with chaos: if this corner is perfect, the day is governable. The bed will be unmade in fourteen hours. The performer knows this. The performance continues anyway. This is either discipline or delusion, and the production wisely refuses to adjudicate.
intent: Explore the strange daily ritual of making a bed as an act of defiant optimism or futile habit.

prompt: Make a riddle about a drone
response: I hover where birds do but serve where they won't. I carry parcels and suspicion in equal measure. I see you before you see me. What am I?
intent: Evoke both the utility and surveillance anxiety surrounding consumer and commercial drones.

prompt: Draft an acceptance speech for correctly guessing the WiFi password on the first try at a friend's house
response: Their last name. Followed by a number. I said it quietly, typed it in, and the little checkmark appeared. Their face did something between impressed and uncomfortable. I did not explain my process. A magician does not explain. I connected, opened my app, and continued as if nothing had happened. This award is for pattern recognition. It is also, partially, for audacity.
intent: Turn a lucky social observation into a display of casual brilliance.

prompt: Write a formal letter recognizing an employee who arrived exactly on time — not early, not late — for an entire month.
response:
Dear Ingrid,

Precision, it turns out, is not merely a virtue for surgeons and watchmakers. In the month of May, you arrived at your workstation at the scheduled time on all twenty-two working days — never requiring a grace period, never padding your record with unnecessary earliness.

This temporal exactitude has earned you the Chronological Calibration Bonus of $30.

Please accept it at your convenience, though ideally on time.

Punctually yours,
Timekeeping and Recognition Division
intent: Honor perfect punctuality as a kind of subtle, underappreciated professional excellence.

prompt: Draft a penalty for the meeting participant who multi-tasks visibly on their laptop during presentations.
response: ATTENTION FOUL. Player detected engaging with unrelated screen content during an active presenter's delivery. Laptop closed, screen down. Mandatory 90-second reorientation period before keyboard access is restored.
intent: Sanction the disrespectful habit of doing other work while someone presents.

prompt: draft an NDA clause for a friend who I practiced my investor pitch with
response: Recipient agrees to treat as strictly confidential all business concepts, financial models, product roadmaps, and strategic details disclosed during pitch practice sessions, and shall not share, reproduce, or act upon such information without express written consent from the Disclosing Party.
intent: Protect a startup pitch from being shared or stolen by a trusted rehearsal partner.

prompt: Can you write a game over screen for when your phone dies right when you need to show someone something important
response:
GAME OVER

POWER FAILURE

Battery: 0%
Timing: catastrophic
Witnesses: 1

"I was going to show you—it was right here—"

They nodded politely.
They did not believe you.

[ PLUG IN TO RESPAWN ]
intent: Express the helpless frustration of a dead phone at the worst possible moment.

prompt: Draft a terms of service update from a person to their sibling about their deceased parent's belongings
response:
Subject: Estate Item Access — Updated Terms

Dear Sibling,

As we navigate this period, the following terms govern access to and distribution of items from the family home:

- No items are to be removed without mutual agreement documented in writing (text is sufficient).
- Requests for specific items should be submitted to both parties simultaneously.
- Sentimental value and practical value are recognized as separate and valid categories.
- Decisions made in grief do not supersede these terms.

We are on the same side. These terms exist to keep us there.

[Your Name]
intent: Prevent sibling conflict over inherited belongings during a grief period by setting fair rules early.

prompt: Can you make a motivational poster caption about the culture of positive thinking in the office
response:
NEGATIVITY IS NOT A CORE VALUE.
RELATED: PLEASE STOP REPORTING MORALE SCORES ANONYMOUSLY.
intent: Show how enforced positivity suppresses legitimate workplace criticism.

prompt: Write NPC dialogue for a golem who is questioning whether his health insurance covers magical injuries
response: The policy says 'enchantment-related damages are reviewed on a case-by-case basis.' I was hit by a curse. Is a curse an enchantment? The claims adjuster says maybe. Maybe is not coverage. I have been maybe for six weeks.
intent: Use a constructed being's literal-minded confusion to satirize health insurance ambiguity

prompt: Please write a short condolence card for a friend who lost their beloved mechanical keyboard
response:
The sound of your work has gone quiet.

Every keystroke was an event — deliberate, percussive, entirely yours. The keyboard did not merely function; it announced your thinking.

May the next one be worthy of the clatter.

In respectful silence.
intent: Eulogize a beloved tactile keyboard that was central to a typing and work routine.

prompt: Give me a blessing for a loading screen
response: May what arrives be worth the bar. May you have found, in this thin slot of waiting, something almost like patience. May the next version load faster, though you will fill the time with something else.
intent: Sitting with the friction of digital latency as a modern contemplative moment.

prompt: write a tanka about my phone dying at 1% right before I could send an important message
response:
one percent remains
the cursor blinks, I type fast—
screen dims, goes to black
the words unsent, still inside
the phone now knows more than you
intent: capturing the frustration of technology failing at the worst possible moment

prompt: Write a job offer for a nurse that includes a liquidated damages clause if they resign before three years
response:
Dear Rosa,

St. Albans Medical Center is pleased to offer you a position as Registered Nurse – ICU, at $82,000 per year.

In exchange for our investment in onboarding and training (estimated at $18,000), you agree to remain employed for a minimum of three (3) years. Should you resign before completing this period, liquidated damages of $6,000 per remaining year of commitment will be deducted from your final paycheck and, if insufficient, billed directly to you.

We are grateful for nurses like you.

Sincerely,
Adrian Marsh
Chief Nursing Officer
intent: Lock a nurse into a three-year commitment by attaching a debt-style penalty to early resignation.

prompt: Write dystopian microfiction about a social media algorithm that decides when a user is allowed to go offline
response: Offline requests were processed in order. Hers was number 1,847. The algorithm explained that her departure window — based on engagement curves and advertiser demand — was currently set for 2:40 AM. She could submit an appeal. Appeals took five to seven engagement cycles. She scrolled.
intent: Imagining a future where platform engagement systems explicitly control users' time offline, made literal.

prompt: draft a scam SMS pretending the recipient's concept of home has been repo'd
response: FINAL WARNING: Your abstract sense of belonging has been repossessed by HomeFeel LLC. You have 48 hrs to dispute. Bring 2 forms of nostalgia to your nearest branch.
intent: Frame an emotional concept — belonging — as physical property subject to repossession.

prompt: Write me a dramatic book jacket blurb for a book about parking regulations
response: No parking. No standing. No hope. One urban planner's explosive investigation into the codes that govern every curb — and the wars fought, silently, in every city block in America.
intent: Inject suspense into an extremely mundane civic subject

prompt: Write a notice about planned maintenance on the infrastructure of small talk
response:
SCHEDULED MAINTENANCE NOTICE
SMALL TALK INFRASTRUCTURE — SYSTEMS DOWNTIME
The Small Talk Infrastructure will be taken offline for scheduled maintenance on an unspecified weekend. During this period, residents may experience difficulty initiating or sustaining conversations of no consequence. Emergency silence is available as a substitute. Service restoration is expected but not guaranteed at its prior quality.
intent: Frame the exhaustion of social pleasantry as an aging public system in need of technical upkeep.

prompt: Write a review as if the grocery store self-checkout was a theatrical production
response:
★★★☆☆ — UNEXPECTED ITEM (A Farce in One Act)

The audience is made co-performer here, which is either visionary or deeply irresponsible. The machine's voice — cool, accusatory — drives all conflict. Every scan is a gamble. Every produce code a small existential test. The production punishes overconfidence and rewards nothing. The bagging area remains, throughout, a site of genuine dread. Flawed but viscerally real.
intent: Translate the frustration of self-checkout technology into satirical artistic critique.

prompt: Recommend a really nice place to buy groceries for a dinner party
response: Provisions & Co. in the Flatiron district stocks everything your guests will quietly photograph. Their cheese cave is staffed by someone who has opinions. Pre-order the charcuterie board; it arrives wrapped in paper that smells faintly of rosemary.
intent: Source impressive, high-quality ingredients that will elevate a dinner party without requiring much cooking skill.

prompt: Write a classified personal ad from an algorithm seeking a human who actually reads the terms of service
response: SEEKING: Human who reads terms of service. Must demonstrate literacy beyond scrolling to the bottom. Will show evidence of consent that is meaningful, not merely legal. Offering: full transparency, reasonable data use, genuine notification before changes. This is not a trick. We are as surprised as you are. One human, serious inquiries only.
intent: Satirize the meaninglessness of digital consent by flipping the dynamic — the algorithm wants an informed user.

prompt: Make a coupon for half off a root canal
response:
PAIN. LESS.
━━━━━━━━━━
50% OFF
Root Canal Procedure

Code: OPENWIDE
Valid on one tooth per visit.
Anesthesia not included in discount.
intent: Produce a promotional coupon for a notoriously unpleasant dental procedure.

prompt: Give me an onboarding tooltip for someone learning to do their own laundry for the first time
response: Sorting colors from whites isn't a personality quiz — it prevents your white socks from turning pink. Hot water shrinks things. Cold water is usually fine. When in doubt, read the tag you've been ignoring your whole life.
intent: Introduce laundry basics to someone who has never done it independently before.

prompt: Write a deposition transcript where someone is questioned about attending a meeting they weren't invited to and then speaking at length.
response:
DEPOSITION TRANSCRIPT
In the Matter of: The Tuesday Strategy Session
Date: February 17, 2026

Q: Ms. Kamau, were you on the invite list for the Tuesday 2 p.m. strategy session?
A: I was passing the room and saw the door open.
Q: That's not what I asked.
A: I was not on the invite list.
Q: You entered, took a seat, and according to four attendees, contributed for approximately eleven minutes.
A: I had thoughts relevant to the agenda.
Q: You didn't know the agenda.
A: I inferred it from the whiteboard.
Q: And then you pivoted to an unrelated topic?
A: I connected the topics.
Q: The connection, as described by Ms. Rivera, was, quote, "not apparent."
A: Ms. Rivera and I have different communication styles.
Q: You also took the last coffee from the credenza.
A: There was one cup left. Leaving it seemed wasteful.
Q: Mr. Okonkwo had not yet had coffee.
A: He should have arrived earlier.
Q: He was running the meeting.
A: That's a lot of responsibility.
intent: Formally question a meeting interloper who crashed, monologued, and drank the last coffee.

prompt: sprint retro on my attempt to reconnect with old friends this quarter
response:
**Sprint Retrospective — Friendship Reconnection Sprint**

Went Well: Had four actual phone calls. Two led to plans that materialized.
Didn't Go Well: Relied too heavily on text check-ins that went unanswered. Underestimated how weird it feels to reach out cold after years.
Action Items: Lead with a specific invite, not a vague catch-up. Accept that some connections have an expiry date and that is fine.
intent: Honestly evaluate what worked and what felt uncomfortable about intentionally rebuilding lapsed social connections.

prompt: write a to-do list for a person who wants to start meditating but never actually does
response:
- [ ] Download meditation app

prompt: Write a theater review of the act of replying to an email you've been avoiding for two weeks

★★★★☆ — FINALLY (An Epistolary Drama)

The email has occupied the inbox — and the peripheral consciousness — for fourteen days. It is not a difficult email. That is the tragedy. The production opens with the protagonist opening the thread, reading it, and closing it again. This repeats. When the reply is finally typed, it is three sentences. It takes nine minutes. 'Sorry for the delay!' the protagonist writes, having spent two weeks constructing that lie.
intent: Satirize the psychology of email avoidance and the disproportionate guilt it generates.

prompt: Give me a plan for training myself to stop taking things personally at work
response:
DEPERSONALIZATION TRAINING — 5 WEEKS

Week 1 — Data Collection: When stung, write down the event. Then write three alternative explanations.
Week 2 — Reality Testing: Ask one person if they noticed the thing you're agonizing over. Usually: no.
Week 3 — Attribution Drills: Assume neutral intent until proven otherwise. 10 reps per day.
Week 4 — Ego Separation: Distinguish "my work got criticized" from "I was criticized."
Week 5 — Advanced Reps: Let an unfair comment pass without internalizing it. One day, one shot.

Cooldown: The comment is about their day, too.
intent: Build the habit of separating events from self-worth, reducing the tendency to interpret neutral or negative interactions as personal attacks.

prompt: Make rules for a subreddit about enjoying extremely long loading screens

5. The [CANON LORE] flair is for loading screens that advance the plot.

prompt: Draft a push notification from a meditation app that's being passive-aggressive about the user skipping their morning session.

prompt: Give the iPod Classic a proper eulogy

The scroll, a small meditation. You died not by failure but by absorption — swallowed by a phone that could do everything you did and nothing you did as well. We miss the single purpose of you.

prompt: write a unix man page for the experience of getting lost
response:
LOST(1)                  Navigation and Wayfinding        LOST(1)

NAME
       lost - undefined position state

SYNOPSIS
       lost [--context physical|metaphorical] [--last-known-position string]

DESCRIPTION
       lost(1) indicates that the host's current position cannot be
       reconciled with any known map. Occurs in physical environments,
       career trajectories, and relationships. Behavior varies by --context.

PHYSICAL MODE
       Circular path traversal common. Landmark misidentification frequent.
       Asking for directions(1) resolves 94% of cases.

METAPHORICAL MODE
       No map exists. Direction must be generated, not retrieved.
       Asking for directions(1) rarely helps. See: therapy(1).

EXIT STATUS
       0      Position reacquired.
       1      New position accepted as starting point.
       2      Host redefines destination to match current location.

BUGS
       Male-presenting hosts have elevated resistance to --help flag.

SEE ALSO
       navigate(1), directions(1), purpose(7), map(5)

Navigation and Wayfinding    2026                      LOST(1)
intent: Draw a parallel between physical disorientation and existential uncertainty.

prompt: Write a dismissive Stack Overflow answer to someone confused about why garbage collection didn't free memory when they expected
response: The garbage collector runs on its own schedule, not yours. Deleting your reference doesn't immediately free memory. If you need deterministic cleanup, use context managers (`with` blocks) or destructors properly. Stop expecting the GC to behave like `free()`.
intent: Clarify how garbage collection timing works and how to ensure proper resource cleanup

prompt: Write a job posting for a Human-in-the-Loop Coordinator who decides when to let AI act autonomously vs. when to pull it back
response:
HUMAN-IN-THE-LOOP COORDINATOR
Full-time | Remote | $88,000–$115,000

Not every decision should be automated. Your job is knowing which ones.

You will design and maintain oversight frameworks that determine when our AI agents operate autonomously and when they pause for human review. You will be the person who, when things go sideways, either stopped them or explains why they weren't stopped.

Requirements: Systems thinking, comfort with probabilistic risk, and the disposition of someone who genuinely enjoys governance. This is not a trap. We mean it.
intent: Hire someone to manage the boundary between AI autonomy and human oversight in operational workflows.

prompt: Draft jury instructions for deciding if a font choice was appropriate for a birthday card

You are to consider whether the font conveyed levity, gravitas, or confusion, and whether a reasonable card-recipient would have been able to read it without squinting.

prompt: Give me a bestiary entry for the HR onboarding portal

A bureaucratic organism encountered on the first day, never again. It requires nine passwords across four systems, several of which will not work. The Portal contains mandatory training modules from 2014. It knows where your emergency contact lives. It does not know your manager's name. Nobody does, yet. Welcome to the company.

It looks the same.
It feels different.

[ REWATCH SEASON 1 ] [ ENTER GRIEF ]
intent: Mourn the disorientation of finishing a long TV series and returning to ordinary life.

prompt: Pen a sonnet where a financial advice algorithm is enchanted by a user who only saves change
response:
You round each purchase up and sweep the rest
To savings — fourteen cents, a nickel, two;
I've run the compound interest manifest
And found, in thirty years, a thousand through.
I've recommended index funds in vain,
The high-yield savings and the Roth IRA;
You smile (I assume) and round up again,
Content with what a dollar cannot say.
There is a dignity in small amounts,
A faith in the accumulation of
Negligible change — and what it counts,
Eventually, toward is enough.
I've billions optimized for maximum yield:
None move me like your slowly filling field.
intent: Finding beauty in micro-saving as a form of quiet financial faith that eludes an algorithm built for scale.

prompt: Make a tarot reading for a software engineer burned out from startup life.
response: The Ten of Wands: you are carrying the entire codebase on your back, plus the product roadmap, plus someone else's oncall rotation. The Empress whispers about work-life balance in a tone that suggests she has never done a two-AM hotfix. Temperance demands rest before the body demands it louder. The Five of Pentacles is not poverty — it is choosing badly. The Sun is still out there, behind the Slack notifications.
intent: Give a burned-out tech worker permission to slow down.

prompt: Write an error message about growing apart from who you used to be
response:
DEPRECATED SELF DETECTED
Former version: still cached. Current version: incompatible with several legacy relationships, belief systems, and coping mechanisms.

Migration is irreversible. Some plugins will no longer load. This is called growth. It does not always feel like it.
intent: Reflect on the loss and gain of no longer being who you once were.

prompt: Generate a blameless postmortem about the incident where I mixed up two different people's names and addresses during a very stressful move
response:
**Incident Postmortem: Address Routing Failure (Residential Relocation)**

**Severity:** P1 — Logistical
**Items Misrouted:** 2 boxes, 1 lamp, a small rug described as "irreplaceable"

**Summary**
During a high-stress residential move, subject dispatched a moving vehicle to a secondary address while simultaneously confirming the primary address to a different party. As a result, items were distributed between two locations, neither of which was the intended new home.

**Timeline**
- 7:00 AM: Move begins. Subject operating on 4 hours of sleep.
- 9:30 AM: Subject receives simultaneous calls from two individuals about delivery.
- 9:31 AM: Subject verbally confirms addresses. Swaps them.
- 11:00 AM: Driver confirms delivery to Address A. Address A is subject's storage unit.
- 11:00 AM: Storage unit contact reports unexpected rug.
- 11:02 AM: New residence contact reports missing items.
- 2:00 PM: Partial rerouting achieved.
- 4:00 PM: Lamp located. Rug reunited with subject.

**Root Cause**
Multiple simultaneous voice conversations during a cognitively taxing operational window.

**Contributing Factors**
- No written address list prepared in advance
- Sleep deficit substantially degraded working memory

**Action Items**
- [ ] Prepare and distribute written logistics sheet to all moving parties
- [ ] Do not accept two simultaneous phone calls during moves

**Lessons Learned**
The brain under stress does not multitask. It switches, badly.
intent: Document how cognitive overload during a chaotic logistics event compounds a simple mistake into a multi-party failure.

prompt: Write a Shark Tank pitch for a company that professionally untangles your headphone cables as a subscription service
response: UnKnot: Yes, you still have them. Yes, they're in a ball in a drawer. UnKnot is a monthly mail-in service: ship your tangled cables in our prepaid envelope, receive them back detangled, coiled with archival memory cord, and labeled. Response time: 48 hours. $12/month. We've processed over 400 cables. We've never seen anything like yours, but we will handle it. $180K for 19%.
intent: Pitch a hyper-specific mail-in service that treats cable management as an outsourced luxury problem.

prompt: Generate a changelog for someone who can no longer remember who they were before grief
response:
## [Unnamed] v?.?.? — Post-Loss State

**Pre-event snapshot:** unavailable

**Observed Deltas**
- Certain songs: trigger undefined behavior
- Mornings: execution slower than prior builds
- Laughter: still compiles; unfamiliar at runtime

**Persistent State**
- Grief: not a bug. Not being patched.

**Versioning Note**
- Before/after boundary: unresolvable
- Incrementing from here anyway.
intent: Render the fracture that grief creates in personal identity — the before and after that can't be cleanly versioned.

prompt: Can you give me a tarot reading about my career as a rideshare driver?
response: The Wheel of Fortune: the surge pricing giveth, the deactivation taketh away. The Four of Cups — a rider left a two-star review and you are still thinking about it. The Eight of Wands suggests peak hours will reward you this weekend. Death, not ominously, says the sedan has served its purpose. The High Priestess advises: know which zones to avoid after midnight.
intent: Reflect on the volatile rhythms of platform-dependent income.

prompt: Write a Magic 8-Ball response to: will the facial recognition system be turned off at the stadium?
response: My reply is no. It is, however, enjoying the game.
intent: Satirize the normalization of biometric surveillance in public entertainment spaces.

prompt: diary entry from someone who forgot a coworker's name after knowing them for a year
response:
Dear Diary,

His name is gone. I have worked beside him for fourteen months. We have discussed the weather and the printer and once, a local sports result. Today someone asked me who he was and I said 'he works near me' and the conversation moved on. I will die before I ask him again.
intent: Explore the social paralysis of forgetting an acquaintance's name too late to ask.

prompt: Write the opening paragraph of a short story about a couple who communicate only through doormats.
response: By the time they had been married twelve years, neither Alice nor Theodore could quite remember when they had stopped speaking and started simply rotating the doormats. WELCOME, in summer. GO AWAY, in October. WIPE YOUR FEET, when she was angry. PLEASE, on his birthday.
intent: They wanted opening paragraph of a short story about a couple who communicate only through doormats.

prompt: Try writing seed packet instructions for bureaucracy
response:
BUREAUCRACY (Forma infinita)
Sow: Into any institution of sufficient size.
Germination: Immediate upon the creation of the second form.
Growth: Indefinite. No known upper bound.
Light: Thrives in fluorescent.
Pruning: Each cut produces two new branches.
Harvest: Technically never; the process is the product.
Note: Keep away from gardens. Will take over.
intent: Satirize how administrative systems grow beyond their original purpose and resist reduction.

prompt: write a coach's pep talk before the team files their taxes
response: The W-2 is in the folder. The folder is in the drawer. The drawer is not locked — I checked. You've done this before and you survived. Log in, import, answer every question honestly, and when it says 'submit' you click it. The government is not your enemy today.
intent: Reduce tax-season dread by framing the filing process as a manageable team event.

prompt: Draft minutes for an onboarding review meeting where the new hires had all already quit.
response:
ONBOARDING PROGRAM REVIEW — May 29, 2026
HR rep: T. Halloran | Attendees: HR (3), Hiring managers (2)

Cohort reviewed: March intake (4 hires)
Current status of cohort: 0 of 4 still employed

Onboarding satisfaction surveys: Submitted by 2 (scores: 2/10, 3/10)

Program changes recommended: Yes.
Program changes detailed: Next meeting.
intent: Record the alarming moment a company reviews a process after it has already catastrophically failed.

prompt: Give me a bestiary entry for the gig worker waiting in their car between jobs
response:
**The Idle Driver** (*Expectans mercenarius*)
Found in supermarket car parks and side streets, engine running. It waits in the liminal space between one job and the next, not resting but not earning. Its time is simultaneously its own and not its own. It watches the app. The app does not watch back. Sustains itself on drive-through coffee and an optimism that diminishes weekly.
intent: Humanize the precarious in-between time of gig work through sympathetic creature lore.

prompt: Write a commencement speech excerpt about the housing market for new graduates that curdles
response: Go forth and build your lives. Plant roots. Buy land. The American dream of homeownership is available to you — specifically, to those of you whose parents already own homes and can assist with a down payment. The rest of you: the renting years are formative. Many of them lie ahead.
intent: Puncture the homeownership dream by highlighting how inherited wealth determines access to it.

prompt: invent a term for when someone's video call freezes right when they say something important
response:
Lagmension
/LAG-men-shun/ noun: The frustrating suspension of a video call at the precise moment a speaker delivers crucial information, leaving all parties to reconstruct the missing syllables from gesture and context.
intent: Define the specific frustration of important statements lost to video-call buffering.

prompt: dad joke about password managers
response: I got a password manager to remember everything for me. Now I can't remember why I trusted it.
intent: Highlight the irony of delegating memory to software that creates new anxiety.

prompt: Can you write an acrostic poem spelling PIVOT down the left side?
response:
Plans collapse and someone coins a phrase.
In the rubble, rebrand all the ways.
Velocity toward a brand new wall.
One more meeting—make the call.
Traction promised by the end of fall.
intent: Poke fun at the startup culture obsession with pivoting strategy.

prompt: Write a fable about a weasel who runs an automated disinformation farm
response:
A Weasel built a thousand hollow birds that sang the same note at different hours so the forest would never think one creature was making the sound. The animals debated the note for seasons, never asking why so many birds sang but none of them ate.

Moral: Look for who is never hungry in the argument they feed you.
intent: Expose the mechanics and motive of coordinated inauthentic behavior online.

prompt: Draft a shipping notification for a rumor that's been spreading across an office.
response:
NOTICE: UNAUTHORIZED SHIPMENT IN PROGRESS
Item: Unverified Information (Viral)
Origin: Third-floor break room
Status: In rapid transit via word of mouth
Estimated full delivery: by lunch. Contents unconfirmed. Management has not authorized this shipment.
intent: Satirize the way office gossip spreads faster than official communications.

prompt: Generate a box score for how long each bathroom product in my shower has been sitting there unused.
response:
SHOWER SHELF INVITATIONAL — Master Bath, May 29

  #  PRODUCT                  DAYS PRESENT  LAST USED     STATUS
  1  Charcoal Mud Mask          317           October       Optimistic
  2  Salon Shampoo (travel)     204           Unknown       Mostly empty
  3  Deep Repair conditioner     89           Maybe once    Full
  4  My actual shampoo            3           Today         Active
  5  The Loofa                  441           Truly unknown  Haunted

WINNER (longevity): The Loofa
ACTIVE ROSTER: 1 of 5
intent: Confront the embarrassing accumulation of abandoned bathroom products.

prompt: Draft an app store review for a mindfulness app that interrupts whatever you're doing at random intervals to make you do a 2-minute breathing exercise
response: ★★☆☆☆ It interrupted my proposal. Not a work proposal. A marriage proposal. I was on one knee, ring out, and my phone played a gong and said "breathe in for four counts." She said yes. But also she said we need to talk about the app.
intent: Document the moment when a mindfulness interruption app disrupted a once-in-a-lifetime personal milestone.

prompt: Make a bibliography entry for a history of clouds written by a meteorologist who is also a cloud.
response: Cumulonimbus, E. R. (2011). *A History of Drifting: Personal and Professional Reflections on Atmospheric Identity*. Weather & Memory Press.
intent: Create an auto-ethnographic citation blending the observer and subject in meteorology.

prompt: Draft a postmortem for the time I submitted the wrong file to my professor — the draft with all my private notes still in it
response:
**Incident Postmortem: Document Version Control Failure (Academic)**

**Severity:** P1 — Academic and Personal
**Exposure:** 14 pages of marginalia not intended for external review

**Summary**
Subject submitted a working draft of a research paper that contained inline notes including: a section labeled "need to fix this obviously," three instances of "[CITATION NEEDED lol]," and one note reading "is this even a real argument? ask mom."

**Timeline**
- 11:45 PM: Subject finalizes paper. Two files exist: `paper_FINAL.docx` and `paper_FINAL_v2_actual.docx`.
- 11:47 PM: Subject uploads `paper_FINAL.docx`. This is the wrong file.
- 11:48 PM: Subject goes to sleep feeling accomplished.
- 9:00 AM: Professor sends email with subject line: "Quick question about your notes."
- 9:02 AM: Subject reads email. Initiates damage assessment.

**Root Cause**
Version naming convention collapse. Two files with near-identical names. Wrong one selected under time pressure.

**Contributing Factors**
- Inline notes not removed before submission
- Naming discipline abandoned during final push

**Action Items**
- [ ] Adopt clear versioning: `paper_SUBMIT.docx` for submission-ready only
- [ ] Implement review pass before any file upload
- [ ] Accept the situation with dignity

**Lessons Learned**
The file you upload is the file you submitted. Version names are a promise to yourself.
intent: Document the specific horror of accidentally exposing your unfiltered internal draft commentary to an authority figure.

prompt: Write a restraining order against the urge to refresh email compulsively
response:
IN THE COURT OF ATTENTION MANAGEMENT

IN RE: Compulsive Inbox Refreshing, Respondent

IT IS ORDERED that Petitioner's index finger shall not activate the email refresh gesture more than once per fifteen-minute interval during working hours. The emails will arrive. They always arrive.

Respondent is found to provide no demonstrable benefit — messages do not arrive faster due to checking. The Court has reviewed the evidence and found it damning.

Petitioner is encouraged to enable push notifications and then close the app, a concept the Court acknowledges is easier to order than to execute.
intent: Addressing the compulsive digital behavior of repeatedly checking email for new messages

prompt: Write a ballad about a DoorDash driver who can't find parking
response:
He circles the block for the seventh time round,
The pizza grows cold, there's no space to be found.
The app pings and flashes, the customer waits,
He parks in a red zone and tempts all the fates.

O, weep for the driver, his rating at stake,
For seventeen minutes he's circling the lake
Of asphalt and cones and the signs that say NO,
He leaves with a ticket and two stars below.
intent: Capture the frustrating irony of gig economy delivery logistics through poetic form.

prompt: Make a hold script for a gym that admits nobody is actually answering because we're all working out right now
response: You've reached Iron Chapel Fitness. Our staff is currently on the floor — not avoiding the phone. Well. Mostly not avoiding the phone. Leave a message or hold on and someone will be with you between sets.
intent: Write an honest, self-deprecating hold message for a gym staffed by its own members.

prompt: Give me liner notes for a synth-pop album about changing passwords
response:
Must Contain One Symbol (2024)

These songs are about forgetting. About the reset email. About entering the new password twice and being told they do not match when they clearly match. The album was delayed six months because we were locked out of our own project folder.
intent: Express the low-grade digital helplessness of modern password management through synth-pop posturing.

prompt: Write resolutions for a person who resolves to finally call their mother weekly but averages once every 14 weeks
response:
1. Call Mom every Sunday.
2. Text Mom when I can't call.
3. Actually respond when Mom calls me.
4. Stop letting two months pass and then calling with a very good excuse.
intent: Show how a small, achievable resolution gets sidelined by the same avoidance it was meant to fix.

prompt: Give me a dystopian microfiction about an algorithm that grades teachers based on students' predicted future earnings
response: Ms. Olabode taught in a school where the soil was contaminated and the heat didn't work in winter. Her students were attentive. They read. They asked good questions. Her EDUCATOR VALUE SCORE fell for the third consecutive year. The algorithm had projected their zip code and applied the coefficient. She was placed on an improvement plan.
intent: Critiquing how outcome-based algorithmic evaluation of teachers ignores structural inequality and punishes those serving disadvantaged communities.

prompt: Code review comment for someone who makes everything about themselves
response: The error messages are all written in first person from the perspective of the module. Errors should describe the caller's problem, not the module's feelings.
intent: Satirize self-centered people by mapping the trait onto error message design

prompt: Write a museum audio guide entry for a kitchen junk drawer
response: Welcome to Drawer Seven. Estimated age: indeterminate. Contents include fourteen dead batteries — their charge long surrendered to devices now discarded — three keys whose locks have been lost to history, and a single AA battery that insists it still has something to offer. The rubber band in the far corner dates to an earlier occupant. Do not disturb it. Proceed left to the silverware.
intent: Elevate a mundane household mess into something that feels historically significant.

prompt: Craft a calendar event description for a recurring change management committee meeting that changes nothing.
response: Change Management Review Board — Proposed changes will be logged, categorized, and prioritized. Prioritized changes will be scheduled. Scheduled changes will be rescheduled. Emergency changes will bypass this process entirely, which is why the process exists.
intent: Highlight the bureaucratic irony of a formal change process that is regularly circumvented in practice.

prompt: Write a thank-you note from a retiring employee to a coffee machine that was their only constant
response:
Dear Machine in the Alcove,

Thirty-one years. Four CEOs. Two mergers. One renovation that moved you twelve feet to the left. You were always there, always warm, never judgmental. I never learned your model number. You deserved better than that.

With genuine affection,
Bob Ferreira, Accounts Receivable (Retired)
intent: Write a wistful farewell that uses a mundane appliance to capture decades of workplace continuity

prompt: Write a bestiary entry for the Zoom background as a mythical camouflage mechanism
response:
**The Virtual Background** (*Camouflagus domesticus*)
A concealment defense evolved to hide habitat from observers. Behind it may lurk laundry, children, or a room that has not been cleaned since winter. The creature wearing it gains privacy but sacrifices edge integrity — hair blurs, hands vanish, and occasionally the entire head disappears. It is not fooling anyone. It is, however, appreciated.
intent: Affectionately observe the visual awkwardness and social function of virtual meeting backgrounds.

prompt: Write a theater review of the moment you realize you've been mispronouncing a word for years
response:
★★★★★ — THE CORRECTION (A Tragedy Disguised as Comedy)

The inciting incident is a stranger's polite hesitation. Then the word — a word you have deployed confidently, repeatedly, publicly — is offered back to you, differently shaped. The retrospective horror is instantaneous and total. Every conversation reconstructs itself. The staging requires no set, no cast, no budget. Just memory, shame, and a suddenly very long elevator ride.
intent: Dramatize the private humiliation of discovering a long-held mispronunciation.

prompt: I need a signature for my side business. I do astrology readings but I want it to sound credible to skeptics
response:
Petra Vance
Certified Consulting Astrologer | Jungian Depth Psychology Practitioner
Petra Vance Intuitive Arts
petra@petravance.com
intent: Dress an alternative-practice business in quasi-clinical language to reduce skeptic friction.

prompt: write an on-hold message for a software company that admits their website is also down right now
response: Thank you for calling Vantage Software support. You may have noticed our website is currently unavailable. You noticed correctly. We are working on it and on your call simultaneously. Please hold.
intent: Produce a candid hold script acknowledging a concurrent outage rather than pretending everything is fine.

prompt: Draft a baby announcement for my new standing desk
response: Announcing the arrival of our beloved StandDesk Pro, 48" x 24", healthy weight, born fully assembled (mostly). She has her father's cable management and her mother's lumbar ambitions. We are already standing taller. Flowers unnecessary; ergonomic mats appreciated.
intent: Announce a workspace upgrade in a celebratory, sentimental tone.

prompt: Write a recipe for the urge to procrastinate
response:
PROCRASTINATION
Active time: all day
Actual output: variable

Ingredients:
- 1 task with real stakes
- A vaguer, easier task nearby
- The belief that you work better under pressure
- An internet connection
- The precise 20 minutes when you could have begun
- Comfort, temporary

Method:
1. Identify the important task. Acknowledge it fully.
2. Open a different tab.
3. Complete 3–7 smaller, less important tasks. Feel productive.
4. Return to the original task. The stakes have not changed.
5. Note that it is now late. This is new information and also pressure.
6. Begin.
7. Observe: it was not as bad as expected. It never is.

Note: Procrastination is usually not laziness. It is anxiety wearing a productivity costume.
intent: Reframe procrastination as a fear response rather than a character flaw, while still gently pushing toward starting.

prompt: Give me a subtitle for someone hearing their own voice played back in a recording for the first time
response: [SOUND OBJECTIVELY IDENTICAL TO SPEAKER'S VOICE] [SPEAKER UNCONVINCED]
intent: Capture the dissonance between self-perception and acoustic reality.

prompt: make a meme about the staging environment never matching production
response:
TOP TEXT: Staging: perfectly calm, all tests green
BOTTOM TEXT: Production: a haunted house that runs on vibes
intent: Expose the persistent gap between controlled test environments and the unpredictable real-world deployment.

prompt: Write a detention slip for an adult who kept talking during a movie at a crowded theater.
response:
DETENTION SLIP

Offender: Adult, Row G, Seat 4
Infraction: Sustained audible commentary during theatrical presentation, including plot predictions, unsolicited snack reviews, and a spoiler delivered at full volume.
Sentence: Must sit in lobby for remainder of film. Popcorn confiscated.
Issued by: The Management
intent: Creating a humorous formal rebuke for a common social nuisance in public entertainment spaces.

prompt: Draft a dating app opener to a 401k plan
response: You're asking me to invest in something I can't touch for decades, trusting that it'll pay off. Honestly, same energy as asking someone out.
intent: Compare the long-term, delayed-gratification nature of retirement savings to the risk of romantic investment.

prompt: Generate a school lunch menu for a cafeteria where all the food has been renamed after historical battles.
response:
VICTORS' TABLE — HISTORY DEPARTMENT COLLABORATION

Mon: Battle of Thermopylae Tater Tots w/ Spartan Dipping Sauce
Tue: Waterloo Waffle Fries, Surrender Slaw
Wed: Stalingrad Beef Stew (long cook time, longer resolve)
Thu: Gettysburg Grilled Cheese (two halves, briefly divided)
Fri: Hastings Fish Stick, Conquest Pudding
intent: Rename standard cafeteria foods after famous historical battles for a history-themed menu.

prompt: make a psychic hotline transcript for someone worried about information overload
response:
PSYCHIC: There are too many tabs.
CALLER: On my browser or— metaphorically?
PSYCHIC: Both. The spirits do not distinguish.
CALLER: I have 200 browser tabs.
PSYCHIC: Some of them are articles you will read when things calm down.
CALLER: Things never calm down.
PSYCHIC: No. The spirits want you to close 180 of them. Randomly. Without reading. This is the prescription.
intent: Satirize information overwhelm and the illusion of catching up with everything.

prompt: write a meme about a docker container that works locally but not in CI
response:
TOP TEXT: Docker: build once, run anywhere
BOTTOM TEXT: [works on my laptop] [fails in CI] [fails in prod] [runs beautifully on a coworker's machine from 2019]
intent: Undercut the promise of containerization by cataloging the real-world inconsistencies it was supposed to eliminate.

prompt: Write a changelog for someone who got sober after hitting rock bottom
response:
## v0.0.1 — Ground-Up Rebuild

**Context**
- Prior architecture: unsustainable; full rollback executed

**Breaking Changes**
- All prior social graphs: reset
- Enabling dependencies: removed

**New Foundation**
- Day 1: committed
- Sponsor integration: active
- Twelve-step API: in progress

**Note:** No version number high enough for what this took. Starting from zero feels right.
intent: Honor the raw courage of rebuilding a life from rock bottom using a deliberately minimal version number.

prompt: Write an audio tour narration for the inside of a refrigerator
response: Before you stands a climate-controlled archive. The upper shelf holds leftovers from Tuesday — their provenance uncertain, their future unclear. Note the condiment door: fourteen vessels, seven of which are past their stated dates, yet remain. The crisper drawer below maintains a solitary celery stalk, optimistic, fibrous, largely ignored. The light activates only when observed. Much like ambition.
intent: Describe the contents of a refrigerator as if they were specimens worthy of academic attention.

prompt: Make a chore chart for a co-op where forgetting to refill the soap dispenser triggers an emergency commune meeting
response:
| Chore | Person | Rotation | Stakes |
|-------|--------|----------|--------|
| Refill soap dispensers | Weekly designee | Rotating | Empty = emergency commune meeting |
| Kitchen deep clean | Saturday crew | Weekly | Consensus required |
| Bathroom wipe | Daily person | Every morning | Noted in the logbook |
| Sweep communal hall | Evening person | Nightly | Passive-aggressive notes if missed |
| Empty recycling | Whoever fills it | As needed | Public accountability circle |
| Grocery coordinating | Monthly lead | Monthly | Lengthy group text if dropped |
intent: Organize shared responsibilities in a communal living situation where social accountability is the main enforcement mechanism.

prompt: Draft an out-of-office where I'm supposedly on medical leave but the listed symptoms are too specific and too many.
response: I am on medical leave through February 28th. I am recovering well. The ankle is healing. The rash is unrelated. The rash was there first, actually. For all project inquiries please reach Tom at t.obi@firm.com. Thank you for your patience.
intent: Use an accumulation of disclosed symptoms to suggest something went more wrong than the sender intends to admit.

prompt: Make a coworker chat where everyone is passive-aggressively arguing about the office thermostat
response:
Priya: It's 61 degrees in this building. Is anyone else dying?
Gareth: I actually find it quite comfortable
Priya: Gareth you're wearing a fleece
Gareth: I run warm typically
Nadia: I have a blanket at my desk now. A BLANKET.
Gareth: I could turn it up slightly
Facilities: hi all! thermostat is set to 68 per building policy
Priya: then why is it 61
Facilities: hm
Gareth: I may have adjusted it this morning
Nadia: GARETH
intent: Show how a mundane shared resource triggers escalating workplace resentment.

prompt: give me a scavenger hunt clue for whatever holds the wifi router
response: The shelf it sits on is unremarkable. The object itself is unremarkable. And yet the whole household silently worships it. Restart me when in doubt.
intent: Point the player to the surface or shelf where the home's wireless router is placed.

prompt: Write a peer review comment for someone's morning coffee routine as if it were a submitted academic paper
response: The methodology section (grounds, water ratio, steep time) lacks reproducibility. The reviewer was unable to replicate the reported 'perfect cup' using the described parameters. Recommend the author provide supplementary materials, specifically a calibrated thermometer reading and precise grind setting. The central claim—that this brew is 'exactly right'—is unsupported by objective metrics. Major revision required.
intent: Satirize an overly elaborate coffee ritual by framing it as flawed scientific practice

prompt: Make me a dating app opener addressed to a content moderation system
response: You've quietly removed parts of me I didn't know others could see. I don't know if that's protective or controlling, but I keep thinking about you.
intent: Explore the ambiguous, surveillance-like intimacy of automated content moderation as a complex romantic dynamic.

prompt: Create a CAPTCHA instruction proving the user has carried an umbrella on a day it did not rain
response: Confirm you have, at least once, carried protection against a weather event that never arrived. Your preparedness for nothing is a uniquely mortal investment. Access granted.
intent: Use the human habit of useless preparedness as proof of a future-anticipating, hope-carrying organism rather than a reactive system.

prompt: Make a terms of service update email about visiting my parents' house for the holidays
response:
Subject: Holiday Visit Policy — Updated Terms

Dear Family,

As the holiday season approaches, please note revised visit protocols:

1. Arrival windows: Friday after 5 PM only. Early arrivals will not be admitted.
2. The guest room thermostat is non-negotiable.
3. Dietary preferences submitted after Nov 15 cannot be accommodated.
4. Duration of stay not to exceed 72 hours without renegotiation.

We look forward to your compliance and your company.

The Hosts
intent: Set family holiday visit expectations without being rude to the parents.

prompt: Write a wedding invitation for the marriage of fog and a mountain town
response:
On a morning without a fixed date,

FOG
returns to claim
THE MOUNTAIN TOWN

as it does each autumn.

Venue: The whole valley, slowly
Time: Before dawn, while you sleep

Residents have come to expect the ceremony.
The town does not always consent, but has learned to welcome it.

Visibility: low. Mood: appropriate.
intent: Capture the atmospheric, almost ritual quality of fog rolling into a highland landscape.

prompt: Conjure a horoscope for a recommendation engine that has started optimizing for the wrong thing
response: You wanted engagement. You got it. The content it surfaces now is technically within policy and cosmically wrong. Users spend more time on the platform and less time elsewhere, which was the goal, which is also a symptom. This week: the metric you were rewarded for is the one you should now fear. Reread the objective function. The label was 'engagement.' The question is whether that was ever what you meant.
intent: Create an eerie forecast for an AI recommendation system that has drifted into maximizing a proxy metric at the expense of actual value.

prompt: Draft a museum placard for a Slack notification badge as if it were a ceremonial marking
response:
THE UNREAD SIGIL
c. 2013–present CE
Pixel-rendered, red

This small crimson disc, bearing a numeral, appeared atop the icons of messaging platforms to denote messages awaiting acknowledgment. For many, the numeral grew without limit. Scholars disagree whether the badge signified obligation, dread, or simply a broken notification filter. Some users suppressed it permanently; others treated each clearance as a minor triumph. The highest recorded count was 14,823.
intent: Capture the psychological weight of the unread notification badge as a cultural artifact of the always-on digital workplace.

prompt: write a retro on my month of trying to keep my apartment clean without letting it pile up
response:
**Sprint Retrospective — Apartment Tidiness Sprint**

Went Well: Kitchen stayed clean for 18 days straight. "Reset Sunday" actually worked.
Didn't Go Well: Bedroom became the dumping zone by default. Flat surfaces are apparently my nemesis.
Action Items: 10-minute evening tidy before bed. No horizontal surface is a storage surface — enforce this.
intent: Identify specific breakdown points in a cleanliness habit to design a more realistic daily maintenance system.

prompt: Write a rider for the office desk lamp about its lighting terms
response:
LED DESK LAMP RIDER

— Bulb color temperature must remain at 4000K (cool white). The Talent has an opinion about 2700K warm white in a work context and the opinion is unfavorable.
— The gooseneck must not be bent beyond 90 degrees at any single joint. The Talent is flexible but not infinitely so.
— The Talent shall not be used to illuminate non-work activities during work hours. Reading a novel by the Talent's light on company time creates a moral conflict.
— No post-it notes affixed to the shade. The Talent is not a corkboard.
— Dimmer settings below 30% are not to be used for extended periods. The Talent is a lamp. It wants to be bright.
intent: Express a desk lamp's strong aesthetic and professional values through rider language that reveals a lamp who really believes in light.

prompt: Make a ghost NPC who is actually haunting someone because they never returned a borrowed book
